L. |journal=Ibis |volume=21 |issue=3 |pages=346–352 }}</ref><ref name= Konig>{{Cite book|title=Owls: A Guide to the Owls of the World|last1=Konig|first1=Claus|last2=Weick|first2=Friedhelm|last3=Becking|first3=Jan-Hendrick|publisher=Yale University Press|year=1999|pages=327–328}}</ref> Barred owls are largely native to eastern [[North America]], but have expanded their range to the west coast of North America where they are considered invasive.<ref name="iucn status" /><ref name= Evers>Evers, L. (2014). ''Beyond anyone's control''. Northwest Science, 88(1), 65–67.</ref><ref name= Kelly>Kelly, E. G. (2001). [https://ir.library.oregonstate.edu/concern/graduate_thesis_or_dissertations/xg94hs869 ''The range expansion of the northern barred owl: an evaluation of the impact on spotted owls'']. Thesis, Oregon State University.</ref> [[Old-growth forest|Mature forests]] are their preferred habitat, but they can also acclimatise to various gradients of open [[woodland]]s.<ref name= BNA>Mazur, K. M. & James, P.C. (2020). "Barred Owl (''Strix varia'')", version 1.0. In ''Birds of the World'' (A. F. Poole and F. B. Gill, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA.</ref> Their diet consists mainly of small [[mammal]]s, but this species is an [[Generalist and specialist species|opportunistic predator]] and is known to prey upon other small [[vertebrate]]s such as [[bird]]s, [[reptile]]s, and [[amphibian]]s, as well as a variety of [[invertebrate]]s.<ref name= Audubon>{{Cite news|url=http://www.audubon.org/field-guide/bird/barred-owl|title=Barred Owl|date=2014-11-13|work=Audubon|access-date=2017-09-01}}</ref>

Barred owls are brown to gray overall, with dark striping on the underside. Barred owls have typical nesting habits for a true owl, tending to raise a relatively small brood often in a [[tree hollow]] or [[Snag (ecology)|snag]] (but sometimes also in other nesting sites) in forested areas.<ref name= Voous>{{cite book | last =Voous | first = Karel H. | author-link = Karel H. Voous |author2 = Cameron, Ad (illustrator) |title = ''Owls of the Northern Hemisphere''| year = 1988 | publisher = London, Collins | isbn =978-0-00-219493-8|pages =225–230}}</ref> As a result of the barred owl's westward expansion, the species has begun to encroach on the range of the related and threatened [[spotted owl]] (''S. occidentalis''). Evidence shows the assorted threats posed by the invading barred species are only increasing. In response, biologists have recommended culling operations to mitigate the negative effect of the barred on the spotted owl species.<ref name= Buchanan>{{cite journal|title=A synopsis of suggested approaches to address potential competitive interactions between Barred Owls (''Strix varia'') and Spotted Owls (''S. occidentalis'')|date=2007 |doi=10.1007/s10530-006-9068-7 |last1=Buchanan |first1=Joseph B. |last2=Gutiérrez |first2=R. J. |last3=Anthony |first3=Robert G. |last4=Cullinan |first4=Tim |last5=Diller |first5=Lowell V. |last6=Forsman |first6=Eric D. |last7=Franklin |first7=Alan B. |journal=Biological Invasions |volume=9 |issue=6 |pages=679–691 |bibcode=2007BiInv...9..679B |s2cid=20935420 }}</ref><ref name= Livezey>{{cite journal|author=Livezey, K. B. |year=2010|title=Killing barred owls to help spotted owls I: a global perspective|journal= Northwestern Naturalist|volume=91|issue=2|pages= 107–133|doi=10.1898/NWN09-37.1 |jstor=40856470|s2cid=11691153 }}</ref>

== Basics ==
[[File:Fulvous Owl (Strix fulvescens) (5957075195).jpg|thumb|Although previously considered a subspecies of barred owl, the [[fulvous owl]] is now considered a distinct species]]

The barred owl was first described by [[Philadelphia]] naturalist [[Benjamin Smith Barton]] in 1799. The species was named due to the varied directions the dusky markings take on their underside.<ref>Barton, B. S. (1883). ''Barton's Fragments of the Natural History of Pennsylvania''. Willughby Society.</ref>

The barred owl is roughly intermediate in size between the larger Ural and the smaller tawny owl, but the structural features of its relatively short and decurved [[claw]]s more so resemble the tawny species as does their dietary and habitat ecology.<ref name= Voous /> The [[spotted owl]] has been hypothesized to be within a [[Species complex|superspecies]] with the barred owl.<ref>Mayr, E., & Short, L. L. (1970). ''Species taxa of North American birds: a contribution to comparative systematics''. Nuttall Ornithological Club.</ref> However, genetic testing reveals very early divergence (likely at or near their ancestor's entry to North America) between spotted and barred owls.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Northern spotted owl (''Strix occidentalis caurina'') genome: divergence with the barred owl (''Strix varia'') and characterization of light-associated genes|date=2017 |pmid=28992302 |pmc=5629816 |doi=10.1093/gbe/evx158 |last1=Hanna |first1=Zachary R. |last2=Henderson |first2=James B. |last3=Wall |first3=Jeffrey D. |last4=Emerling |first4=Christopher A. |last5=Fuchs |first5=Jérôme |last6=Runckel |first6=Charles |last7=Mindell |first7=David P. |last8=Bowie |first8=Rauri C. K. |last9=Derisi |first9=Joseph L. |last10=Dumbacher |first10=John P. |journal=Genome Biology and Evolution |volume=9 |issue=10 |pages=2522–2545 }}</ref> A fossil species once called ''Strix brea'' from the early Pleistocene in California does little to resolve the ancestry of modern species, given its ambiguous relation to any living ''Strix''. The fossil species was larger (more similar in size and slenderness to the spotted owl, albeit with a slightly smaller skull and geographically isolated from that species) and longer-legged than either the spotted and barred owls, and is now considered to be in a separate genus, ''[[Oraristrix]]''.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.3853/j.0067-1975.62.2010.1534|title=A new genus for the extinct late Pleistocene owl ''Strix brea'' Howard (Aves: Strigiformes) from Rancho La Brea, California|journal=Records of the Australian Museum|volume= 62|pages= 123–144|author1=Campbell Jr, K. E.|author2=Bochenski, Z. M. |year=2010|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v035n02/p0066-p0069.pdf|author=Howard, H. |title=A New Species of Owl from the Pleistocene of Rancho la Brea, California |journal=The Condor |date=1933 |volume=35 |issue=2 |pages=66–69 |doi=10.2307/1363650|jstor=1363650 }}</ref> Pleistocene era fossils of probable barred owls are known from Florida, Tennessee and Ontario.<ref>{{cite journal|jstor=4085986| author1=Parmalee, P. W.| author2=Klippel, W. E. |year=1982|journal= The Auk|volume= 99|issue=2|pages= 365–368|title=Evidence of a Boreal Avifauna in Middle Tennessee during the Late Pleistocene}}</ref>

=== Subspecies ===
[[File:Barred Owl (Strix varia) (9732594639).jpg|thumb|left|Some [[subspecies]] such as ''S. v. georgica'' from Florida differ from northern barred owls by their darker brown streaks and, more noticeably, their bare toes]]
[[File:Barred Owl (25840348850).jpg|thumb|right|The Texas subspecies, ''S. v. heveola'', has mostly bare and relatively sizable toes and distinctly cinnamon-hued plumage]]

The subspecies of the barred owl vary mostly by region, with slight to moderate variation by coloring, size and extent of feathering on the toes. Although several have been described in the past, the barred owl may include only three subspecies, subsequent to the separation of the fulvous and cinereous forms.<ref name= Ridgway /><ref>Peters, J. L. (1940). ''Check-list of birds of the world. Vol. 4''. Cambridge: Harvard Univ. Press.</ref>

*''S. v. varia'' (Barton, 1799): The northern barred owl. This race lives throughout the [[Northeastern United States]] and the [[Upper Midwest]], ranging as far south as [[Oklahoma]], the [[Carolinas]] and northern [[Georgia (U.S. state)|Georgia]]. It is also considered to comprise all western "invader" birds found as far west as California and British Columbia.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= BNA /> However, genetic study of westerly birds show a substantial isolation, possibly up to the subspecific level, of the western and eastern populations of the northern owls, with an estimated divergence of around 7,000 years, perhaps indicating an unknown history of the species in remote forests of northern and central Canada (far west of what was considered their original distribution) that radiated more recently to comprise the western populations.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Genomic Variation and Recent Population Histories of Spotted (''Strix occidentalis'') and Barred (''Strix varia'') Owls|date=2021 |doi=10.1093/gbe/evab066 |last1=Fujito |first1=Naoko T. |last2=Hanna |first2=Zachary R. |last3=Levy-Sakin |first3=Michal |last4=Bowie |first4=Rauri C K. |last5=Kwok |first5=Pui-Yan |last6=Dumbacher |first6=John P. |last7=Wall |first7=Jeffrey D. |journal=Genome Biology and Evolution |volume=13 |issue=5 |pmid=33764456 |pmc=8120011 }}</ref> This race is generally the typical mid gray-brown variety of barred owl.<ref name= Konig /> However, a paler variation with very washed out markings and a more pure whitish base color is known (in eastern Canada such as Quebec), formerly considered a race ''S. v. albescens'', as well as darker and browner variation in northern Minnesota (formerly ''S. v. brunnescens'').<ref name= BNA /><ref>{{cite journal|author=Bishop, L. B. |year=1931|title=Three apparently undescribed owls|journal= Proceedings of the Biological Society of Washington|volume= 44|pages= 93–95}}</ref> This race is fairly large (described as the largest race on average with the probable separation of the cinereous owl). The [[Wing chord (biology)|wing chord]] and tail length may measure from {{convert|312|to|340|mm|in|abbr=on}} and {{convert|215|to|230|mm|in|abbr=on}} in males and {{convert|320|to|352|mm|in|abbr=on}} and {{convert|224|to|257|mm|in|abbr=on}} in females.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Ridgway /> One nominate bird had a [[Tibiotarsus|tarsus]] length of {{convert|63.5|mm|in|abbr=on}} and the [[Culmen (beak)|culmen]] from the cere may measure {{convert|23.5|to|30|mm|in|abbr=on}}.
*''S. v. georgica'' ([[John Latham (ornithologist)|Latham]], 1801): The southern barred owl or, alternately, the Florida barred owl. This subspecies is found in southern [[North Carolina]] closer to the coast along to broadly through [[Georgia (U.S. state)|Georgia]] and all of [[Florida]].<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Ridgway /><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/4075950|jstor=4075950 |last1=Wetmore |first1=Alexander |title=The Florida Barred Owl in North Carolina |journal=The Auk |date=1930 |volume=47 |issue=2 |page=253 |doi-access=free }}</ref> This is the smallest of the three subspecies on average.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= BNA /> Known wing chord lengths can vary from {{convert|315|to|357|mm|in|abbr=on}}. Tail length is {{convert|205|to|231|mm|in|abbr=on}} and the bill from the cere is {{convert|23|to|28|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= BNA /> Males in Florida were found to weigh from {{convert|681|to|800|g|lb|abbr=on}}, with averages in two samples of {{convert|718|and|744|g|lb|abbr=on}}, while two females weighed {{convert|850|and|875|g|lb|abbr=on}}, respectively. It therefore appears to show less pronounced [[sexual dimorphism]] than the northern barred owl race.<ref name= Weidensaul />
*''S. v. helveola'' ([[Outram Bangs|Bangs]], 1899): The Texas barred owl. Comprises most barred owls found in Texas.<ref name= Konig /> The distributional range is considered to range as far north as [[Lee County, Texas|Lee County]], east to [[Chambers County, Texas|Chambers County]], west to [[Kerr County, Texas|Kerr County]] and south to [[Nueces County, Texas|Nueces County]].<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Howell>Howell, S. N., & Webb, S. (1995). ''A guide to the birds of Mexico and northern Central America''. Oxford University Press.</ref><ref>Oberholser, H. C. (1974). ''The Bird Life of Texas''. University of Texas Press, Austin, TX, USA.</ref> In this race, the ground coloration tends to pale gingery-cream and the back and head tend to be a pale brown ranging into an almost cinnamon color.<ref name= BNA /> The toes can vary from rather bare to slightly bristled.<ref name= Konig /> This race is similar to other barred owls in size, perhaps averaging marginally smaller than those in the nominate race, but its bill and feet are larger on average than the preceding two races.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Ridgway />

== Description ==
[[File:Barred Owl Closeup.jpg|thumb|left|A barred owl in [[Ontario]]|alt=Close-up, [[Ontario]]]]

The barred owl is considered somewhat subdued and drab in coloration compared to the sometimes rich coloring of other [[Sympatry|sympatric]] owls.<ref name= Hume>Hume, R. (1991). ''Owls of the world''. Running Press, Philadelphia.</ref> Overall, this owl is greyish-brown or brown. The brownish color extends from the head to the back. Barred owls are scalloped with white bars on the mantle and the back, bearing as well some whitish spotting on the [[Covert feather|wing coverts]].<ref name= Voous /><ref name= BNA /> The underside has a pale creamy gray-brown base color (ranging into dirty white in the palest individuals) overlaid about the throat and upper chest with horizontal, slightly crescent-shaped barring (hence its common name), while the belly is boldly streaked in a vertical pattern. The streaking is usually blackish, dusky brown, or sometimes rufescent- (reddish-) brown.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Hume /> The head is fairly large (although not especially so for a species in ''Strix'') and rounded with no [[ear tuft]]s.<ref name= Mikkola>''Owls of the World: A Photographic Guide'' by Mikkola, H. Firefly Books (2012), {{ISBN|978-1-77085-136-8}}</ref><ref name= HBW>Holt, D.W., Berkley, R., Deppe, C., E. Rocha, P., Petersen, J.L., Rangel Salazar, J.L., Segars, K.P., Wood, K.L. & Marks, J.S. (2019). "Typical Owls". In: del Hoyo, J., Elliott, A., Sargatal, J., Christie, D.A. & de Juana, E. (eds.). ''Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive''. Lynx Edicions, Barcelona.</ref> The [[facial disc]] is pale grayish-brown with darker yet subtle [[Concentric objects|concentric lines]].<ref name= Voous /> The [[Beak|bill]] is pale straw-yellow (occasionally showing a mild greenish tint) while the cere (a bare structure at the base of the beak) is "horn-colored".<ref name= Voous /><ref name= Hume /> Its eyes are a dark brown color; the eyes may appear intensely black in the field and, although large, are fairly closely set.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Barred Owl Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology |url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Barred_Owl/id |access-date=2023-12-14 |website=www.allaboutbirds.org |language=en}}</ref><ref name= Hume />

The barred owl has well-developed eye anatomy. As is typical of owls, their ocular anatomy is quite distinct from [[diurnal raptor]]s especially in terms of their [[photoreceptor cell]]s, as they have a very large number of [[rod cell]]s in their quite sensitive [[retina]].<ref name= Weidensaul /><ref name= Braekevelt>{{cite journal|title=Fine structure of the retinal photoreceptors of the barred owl (''Strix varia'')|date=1996 |pmid=8720450 |last1=Braekevelt |first1=C. R. |last2=Smith |first2=S. A. |last3=Smith |first3=B. J. |journal=Histology and Histopathology |volume=11 |issue=1 |pages=79–88 }}</ref> However, their [[pecten oculi]] is smaller relative to the size of their large [[Orbit (anatomy)|ocular globe]] (other large [[Great horned owl|owls]] are known to have similar pecten proportions).<ref name= Braekevelt /> The vision in limited or almost no light during a laboratory study of a barred owl was found to be similar to that of other owls, including the [[long-eared owl]] (''Asio otus'') and the [[American barn owl]] (''Tyto furcata'').<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1086/281276|jstor=2457707 |title=Minimum Intensities of Illumination Under Which Owls Can Find Dead Prey by Sight |date=1945 |last1=Dice |first1=Lee R. |journal=The American Naturalist |volume=79 |issue=784 |pages=385–416 |s2cid=86535713 }}</ref> The tarsi and toes are feathered up to the dark gray, black-tipped talons. These feathers are more sparse and bristled in the southern races. On individuals with bare sections of their toes, the toes are yellowish-gray in color.<ref name= Voous /><ref name="Terres">{{cite book| last =Terres| first =J. K.| author-link =John Kenneth Terres| title =The Audubon Society Encyclopedia of North American Birds| publisher =Knopf| year =1980| location =New York, NY| page =[https://archive.org/details/audubonsocietyen00terr/page/665 665]| isbn =0-394-46651-9| url-access =registration| url =https://archive.org/details/audubonsocietyen00terr/page/665}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/4077355|jstor=4077355 |last1=Kelso |first1=Leon |last2=Kelso |first2=Estelle H. |title=The Relation of Feathering of Feet of American Owls to Humidity of Environment and to Life Zones |journal=The Auk |date=1936 |volume=53 |issue=1 |pages=51–56 |doi-access=free }}</ref> The flight feathers are barred with whitish buff and brown while the tail is brown or grayish-brown with 4–5 whitish bars.<ref name= Voous /> Young barred owls with their second set of [[down feather]]s are fluffy brownish-white, with indistinct darker barring on their head, back and mantle. They quickly become juveniles which resemble adults but have less distinct markings (especially about the head and neck), more buff coloring overall, often some remnant down, pinkish skin and a pale, blue-green cere. Also the tail at this age may have as many as seven bands (though sometimes have four, like adults). Full adult plumage is obtained via [[Moulting|molt]] after about a year as well as adult bare part characteristics.<ref name= Voous /><ref name= BNA /><ref>{{cite journal|author=Shufeldt, R. W. |year=1911|title=Plumages of the Young of the Barred Owl|journal=The Wilson Bulletin|volume= 23|issue=2|pages=113–119|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v023n02/p0115-p0119.pdf}}</ref> A study of tail molt in [[Washington (state)|Washington]] showed that molt tends to occur relatively quickly, and that young individuals are difficult to age by state of molt alone.<ref>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/nabb_35-2_61-68.pdf|author1=Acker, J.|author2= Garcia, D. |year=2010|title=Notes on rectrix molt in Barred Owls (''Strix varia'')|journal=North American Bird Bander|volume= 35|issue=2|page=61}}</ref> Southern barred owls tend to be darker and slightly smaller than northerly ones.<ref>Karalus, K. E. & Eckert, E.W. (1974). ''The owls of North America (north of Mexico)''. Garden City, NY: Doubleday and Co.</ref> Rare captive and wild barred owls with [[albinism]] have been described and are pure white but tend to retain their brown eyes.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Dean, R. |year=1976|title=Albinism and melanism among North American Birds|journal= Bulletin of the Nuttall Ornithological Club|volume= 2|pages= 20–24}}</ref>

[[File:Barred-Owl 9146.jpg|thumb|A barred owl using its tail as an air dam and brake|right|alt=Landing on a fence post]]

The barred owl is a large species. The adult measures anywhere from {{convert|40|to|63|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length while the wingspan may range from {{convert|96|to|125|cm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name="owlpages.com">[http://www.owlpages.com/owls.php?genus=Strix&species=varia Barred Owl (''Strix varia'') – Information, Pictures, Sounds]. The Owl Pages (2015-11-04). Retrieved on 2016-08-01.</ref><ref name= Ridgway>Ridgway, R., & Friedmann, H. (1914). ''The Birds of North and Middle America: A Descriptive Catalog of the Higher Groups, Genera, Species, and Subspecies of Birds Known to Occur in North America, from the Arctic Lands to the Isthmus of Panama, the West Indies and Other Islands of the Caribbean Sea, and the Galapagos Archipelago'' (Vol. 50). US Government Printing Office.</ref> The wing area (measured by square [[centimeter]] relative to the body mass) is quite intermediate among American owls, with the [[Bird flight|wing loading]] being lower than larger, but proportionately small-winged [[Horned owl|larger owls]] and even than some [[Screech owl|smaller owls]]. The barred has high wing-loading. Wing-loading is related to hunting technique, with higher wing-loading owls typically hunting from a perch, with only a brief flight necessary to obtain food, whilst lower wing-loading owls often hunt their prey from active flight. As is the case in most owls, the various wing feathers of barred owls are uncharacteristically soft and bear a [[comb]]-like shape, which in turn renders their flight functionally silent during their hunts.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1093/icb/icy002|title=Barred Owl (''Strix varia'') Feather Pennulae|author1=Liu, L. G.|author2=Le Piane, K.|author3=Clark, C. J. |year=2018|journal= Integrative and Comparative Biology| volume =58 |pages=E364|doi-access=free}}</ref> Like most [[birds of prey]], the female is larger than the male barred owl, sometimes described as [[Sexual dimorphism|reverse sexual dimorphism]] (due to the fact that males average larger than females in most non-raptorial birds).<ref name= Konig />

Among standard measurements, the [[Wing chord (biology)|wing chord]] of grown males varies from {{convert|303|to|340|mm|in|abbr=on}}, with an average from three sources of {{convert|326.2|mm|in|abbr=on}}, the [[tail]] may measure from {{convert|182|to|250|mm|in|abbr=on}}, with an average of {{convert|219.6|mm|in|abbr=on}} and the [[Beak|culmen from the cere]] may measure from {{convert|22|to|27|mm|in|abbr=on}}, with an average of {{convert|24.3|mm|in|abbr=on}}. Meanwhile, for the female, the wing chord may range from {{convert|318|to|357|mm|in|abbr=on}}, averaging {{convert|335.8|mm|in|abbr=on}}, the tail from {{convert|204|to|257|mm|in|abbr=on}}, averaging {{convert|223.3|mm|in|abbr=on}} and the culmen from the cere {{convert|20|to|30.7|mm|in|abbr=on}}, averaging {{convert|25.1|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Ridgway /><ref name="Earhart">{{cite journal |last1=Earhart |first1=Caroline M. |last2=Johnson |first2=Ned K. |date=1970 |title=Size Dimorphism and Food Habits of North American Owls |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v072n03/p0251-p0264.pdf |journal=The Condor |volume=72 |issue=3 |pages=251–264 |doi=10.2307/1366002 |jstor=1366002}}</ref><ref name="Carpenter">{{cite journal |last1=Carpenter |first1=Thomas W. |date=1992 |title=Utility of Wing Length, Tail Length and Tail Barring in Determining the Sex of Barred Owls Collected in Michigan and Minnesota |journal=The Condor |volume=94 |issue=3 |pages=794–795 |doi=10.2307/1369270 |jstor=1369270}}</ref> Sexual dimorphism is particularly pronounced in barred owls by body mass as males within a population are sometimes a third lighter in weight.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Ridgway /> In the nominate subspecies (''S. v. varia''), average weights for males have been reported as {{convert|621.9|g|lb|abbr=on}} (sample size 12), {{convert|632|g|lb|abbr=on}} (sample size 20) and {{convert|681|g|lb|abbr=on}} (sample size unknown) in three samples.<ref name= Ridgway /><ref name= Earhart /><ref name= Weidensaul>Weidensaul, S. (2015). ''Owls of North America and the Caribbean''. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt.</ref> The weight range for adult males is known to vary from {{convert|468|to|812|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Ridgway /><ref name= Earhart /> The considerably larger female of the nominate subspecies has been reported to average {{convert|801|g|lb|abbr=on}} (sample size 24), {{convert|872.6|g|lb|abbr=on}} (sample size 14) and {{convert|909.5|g|lb|abbr=on}} (sample size unknown). Altogether, fully-grown female barred owls may weigh from {{convert|610|to|1150|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Ridgway /><ref name= Earhart /><ref name= Weidensaul />

=== Vocalization ===
The barred owl is a powerful vocalist, with an array of calls that are considered "spectacular, loud and emphatic".<ref name= Bent>Bent, A. C. (1961). ''Life Histories of North American Birds of Prey (part 2), Orders Falconiformes and Stringiformes (Vol. 170)''. US Government Printing Office.</ref> Calls probably carry well over {{convert|0.8|km|mi|abbr=on}}.<ref>Bosakowski, T. (1987). "Census of Barred Owls and Spotted Owls". In ''Biology and Conservation of Northern Forest Owls'': Symposium Proceedings: February 3–7, 1987, Winnipeg, Manitoba (Vol. 142, p. 307). Fort Collins, Colo.: Rocky Mountain Forest and Range Experiment Station.</ref> Its usual call is a series of eight accented hoots ''ok-ok-ok-ok ok-ok-buhooh'', or the "typical two-phrase hoot" with a downward pitch at the end.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Odom>Odom, K. (2009). [https://scholar.uwindsor.ca/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?referer=&httpsredir=1&article=1296&context=etd ''Vocalizations, vocal behaviour, and geographic variation in the calls, duets, and duetting behaviour of a nonpasserine, the Barred Owl (Strix varia)'']. M.S. Thesis, University of Windsor.</ref> The most common mnemonic device for remembering the call is "Who cooks for you, who cooks for you all."<ref name= Voous /> Due to its best known call, the barred owl is sometimes colloquially referred to as ''Old Eight-Hooter''.<ref>Haas, F. C. & Burrows, R. (2005). ''Birds of Pennsylvania''. Lone Pine.</ref> At 80% of study posts in [[Virginia]], barred owls responded to playback of this call.<ref name= Mcgarigal>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/1367961|jstor=1367961 |last1=McGarigal |first1=Kevin |last2=Fraser |first2=James D. |title=Barred Owl Responses to Recorded Vocalizations |journal=The Condor |date=1985 |volume=87 |issue=4 |pages=552–553 }}</ref> A further call is the "ascending type" or the "[[legato]]" call, a series of variable notes ending in ''oo-aw'' or ''hoo-aah''.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Odom /> At least two other variations on the legato/ascending call are known.<ref name= Odom /> 56% of studied owls in Virginia engaged in the ascending type call but 36% uttered only the closing notes.<ref name= Mcgarigal /> The isolated ''hoo-aah'', sometimes called the "inspection call", was the most common song type in north Florida and the most likely to be heard during daylight.<ref name= Odom2>{{cite journal|doi=10.1525/cond.2010.090163 |title=A Quantitative Description of the Vocalizations and Vocal Activity of the Barred Owl |date=2010 |last1=Odom |first1=Karan J. |last2=Mennill |first2=Daniel J. |journal=The Condor |volume=112 |issue=3 |pages=549–560 |s2cid=54987338 |url=https://scholar.uwindsor.ca/biologypub/1028 |url-access=subscription }}</ref> Several other calls, although some are not dissimilar variations on the main calls, are known.<ref name= Mcgarigal /><ref>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v012n04/p0362-p0367.pdf|author1=Brewster, W.|author2=Chapman, F. M. |year=1891|title=Notes on the birds of the lower Suwanee River|journal=The Auk|volume=8|issue=2|pages=125–138|doi=10.2307/4068066 |jstor=4068066 }}</ref><ref>Saunders, A. A. (1951). ''A guide to bird songs''. New York: Doubleday.</ref> Some of these vary into cackles, hoots, caws and gurgles, at times described as "sudden demonic laughter", "cat-like screams" and "prolonged outbursts of cackling" and seem to be, among ''Strix'' species, an idiosyncrasy endemic to the barred owl.<ref name= Voous /><ref name= Bent /><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/4078760|jstor=4078760 |last1=Taber |first1=Wendell |title=The Scream of the Northern Barred Owl |journal=The Auk |date=1940 |volume=57 |issue=2 |page=253 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

Another call type is the "mumble", a grumbling, slurred and subtle ''err-ERR-err'', also an up-and-down "twitter" call at a high pitch.<ref name= Odom /> When agitated, this species will make a buzzy, rasping hiss about three times in three seconds, repeating every 10–30 seconds, and will click its beak together forcefully.<ref name= Bent /> Females and juveniles beg with high scratching ''skreeechch'' notes.<ref name= Konig /> The voice of the two sexes is similar, but the female has a higher-pitched voice with longer terminal notes.<ref name= Odom2 /><ref>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/137289|author1=Bird, D. M.|author2=Bird, J.O. |year=1977|title=Apparent distraction display by a Barred Owl|journal=Canadian Field-Naturalist|volume=91|issue=2 |pages=176–177|doi=10.5962/p.345368 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Of calls, 87 to 94% are identifiable to sex per one study.<ref name= Odom /> While calls are most common at night, the birds do call during the day as well, especially when provoked by human playback or imitation.<ref>{{cite book|last=Alderfer|first=Jonathan|title=National Geographic: Complete Birds of North America |url=https://archive.org/details/nationalgeograph00jona|url-access=registration|year=2006|publisher=National Geographic Society|location=Washington D.C.|pages=330–331|isbn=978-1-4262-0135-6}}</ref> They are more responsive than any hawk in the east to playback of calls of their own species.<ref name= Mosher>{{cite journal|jstor=4513583|author1=Mosher, J. A.|author2=Fuller, M. R.|author3= Kopeny, M. |year=1990|title=Surveying Woodland Raptors by Broadcast of Conspecific Vocalizations |journal=Journal of Field Ornithology|pages=453–461|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jfo/v061n04/p0453-p0461.pdf}}</ref> The barred owl is noisy in most seasons but peak vocalization times for barred owls tend to be between late January (in Florida) and early April (in Canada).<ref name= Odom2 /><ref name= Dunstan>{{cite journal|author1=Dunstan, T. C.|author2=Sample, S. D. |year=1972|title=Biology of barred owls in Minnesota|journal=Loon|volume=44|issue=4|pages=111–115}}</ref><ref name= Taylor>Taylor, P. (1983). ''Wings along the Winnipeg: birds of the Pinawa-Lac du Bonnet region, Manitoba''. Winnipeg, MB: (Eco Series no. 2.) Manitoba Nat. Soc.</ref> Two seasonal peaks in vocalizations, one right before breeding and another after the young have dispersed, were detected in [[Connecticut]], with peak vocalizations on nights with extensive cloud cover.<ref>Yannielli, L. C. (1989). ''Preferred habitat of barred owls (''Strix varia'') in Litchfield County, Connecticut, with additional comments on distribution and abundance''. M.S. thesis, South. Conn. State Univ.</ref> Peak times for vocalizations are between 6:00{{nbsp}}pm and 6:00{{nbsp}}am, with the least frequent vocalizations around mid-afternoon.<ref name= Odom2 />

== Distribution and habitat ==
[[File:Barred-Owl 9052.jpg|thumb|Winter in [[Vermont]]]]

The barred owl is distributed throughout most of the [[eastern United States]], as well as much of [[southern Canada]].<ref name= Konig /> They are found as far northeast as much of [[Nova Scotia]] (western two-thirds), [[New Brunswick]] and [[Sept-Îles, Quebec|Sept-Îles]], in much of [[Quebec]], up to [[Lake Mistassini]], and [[Ontario]], up to [[Moosonee]].<ref name="iucn status" /><ref name= Erskine>{{cite book|author=Erskine, A. J.|year=1992|title=Atlas of Breeding Birds of the Maritime Provinces|publisher=Nova Scotia Museum, Halifax, NS, Canada}}</ref><ref>Gagnon, C. & Bombardier, M. (1996). "Barred Owl." In ''The breeding birds of Quebec: atlas of the breeding birds of southern Québec''., edited by J. Gauthier and Y. Aubry, 598–601. Québec Region, Montréal: Assoc. québecoise des groupes d'ornithologues, Prov. of Quebec Soc. for the protection of birds, Can. Wildl. Serv., Environ. Canada.</ref><ref name= Eagles>Eagles, P. F., Cadman, M. D., & Helleiner, F. M. (1987). ''Atlas of the breeding birds of Ontario''. University of Waterloo Press.</ref><ref name= Peck>Peck, G. K., & James, R. D. (1993). [https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/11-18%20OB%20Vol12%231%20Apr1994.pdf ''Breeding Birds of Ontario: Nidiology and Distribution''. Volume 1: Nonpasserines] (first Revision-part A: loons to ducks). Ontario Birds, 11, 18–22.</ref> The barred owl has been recorded as far north as central [[Labrador]], though the species is not yet confirmed to breed in the province.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.22621/cfn.v118i2.929 |title=First Record of a Barred Owl, ''Strix varia'', in Labrador |date=2004 |last1=Schmelzer |first1=Isabelle |last2=Phillips |first2=Frank |journal=The Canadian Field-Naturalist |volume=118 |issue=2 |page=273 |doi-access=free }}</ref> The barred owl ranges in every part of the [[eastern United States]] continuously from northernmost [[Maine]] down throughout [[New England]], the [[Mid-Atlantic (United States)|Mid-Atlantic states]], much of the [[Midwestern United States|Midwest]], the [[Southeast United States]] and all of [[Florida]].<ref name="iucn status" /><ref name= Weidensaul /> Their range extends without substantial gaps to the limits of western [[Minnesota]], easternmost [[South Dakota]], the southeastern corner of [[Nebraska]], the eastern half roughly of [[Kansas]], most of [[Oklahoma]] and [[east Texas]] to as far west as [[Cisco, Texas|Cisco]] and [[Burnet, Texas|Burnet]].<ref name="iucn status" /><ref>Janssen, R. B. (1987). ''Birds in Minnesota''. University of Minnesota Press, Minneapolis, MN, USA.</ref><ref>Peterson, R. A. (1995). ''The South Dakota Breeding Bird Atlas''. South Dakota Ornithologists' Union, Northern Prairie Wildlife Research Center, Jamestown, ND, USA.</ref><ref>[[Paul Johnsgard|Johnsgard, P. A.]] (1979). ''Birds of the Great Plains: Breeding Species and their Distribution''. University of Nebraska Press, Lincoln, NE, USA.</ref><ref name= Johnsgard>[[Paul Johnsgard|Johnsgard, P. A.]] (1988). ''North American owls: biology and natural history''. Smithsonian Institution.</ref> Arguably and discontinuously from Texas, the species may range into central and southern [[Mexico]] but these populations are now often considered a [[Cinereous owl|separate species]].<ref name="iucn status" /><ref>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jrr/v027n03/p00154-p00160.pdf|author=Holt, D. W. |year=1993|title=Presence and distribution of Mexican owls: a review|journal= J Raptor Res|volume= 27|issue=3|pages= 154–160}}</ref> These initial parts of the range in the eastern and central stretches would be considered as where the species is "native".<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Livezey2>{{cite journal|doi=10.1674/0003-0031-161.1.49 |title=Range Expansion of Barred Owls, Part I: Chronology and Distribution |date=2009 |last1=Livezey |first1=Kent B. |journal=The American Midland Naturalist |volume=161 |pages=49–56 |s2cid=86276981 }}</ref> A wandering barred owl was once seen flying over [[Lake Michigan]] {{convert|48|km|mi|abbr=on}} from the nearest land.<ref>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v059n02/p0111-p0111.pdf|author=Blake, E. R. |year=1947|title=Barred owl thirty miles from land|journal= The Wilson Bulletin|volume= 59|issue=2|page= 111}}</ref>

=== Range expansions ===
[[File:Juvenile barred owl.jpg|thumb|A juvenile barred owl peers out in the verdant forests of [[Washington (state)|Washington]], where the species is an "invasive" recent invader]]

The remaining parts of the range are considered where the barred owl introduced itself in the last century or so. The historical lack of trees in the [[Great Plains]] presumably acted as a barrier to the range expansion, and recent increases in forests broke down this barrier.<ref name= Livezey2 /> Increases in forest distribution along the [[Missouri River]] and its tributaries provided barred owls with sufficient foraging habitat, protection from the weather, and concealment from avian predators. This allowed barred owls to move westward, initially solely along other [[Riparian zone|forested river corridors]] (e.g. the [[Yellowstone River|Yellowstone]] and [[Musselshell River|Musselshell]]), but increases in forests in the northern [[Great Plains]] decades later would allow them to connect their eastern and western distributions across southern Canada. These increases in forests were caused by [[European-American settlers]] via [[wildfire suppression]] and ceasing the fires historically set by [[Indigenous peoples of the Americas|Native Americans]], as well as by increased tree-planting.<ref name= Holt>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/3536484|jstor=3536484 |last1=Holt |first1=Denver W. |last2=Domenech |first2=Rob |last3=Paulson |first3=Anne |title=Status and Distribution of the Barred Owl in Montana |journal=Northwestern Naturalist |date=2001 |volume=82 |issue=3 |pages=102–110 }}</ref>

In Canada, the barred owls with range expansion now range through southern [[Manitoba]] (excluding the southwest corner), a broad section of south-central [[Saskatchewan]], and east-central and nearly all of western [[Alberta]], now up to [[High Level, Alberta|High Level]].<ref name="iucn status" /><ref name= Duncan>{{cite journal|author=Duncan, J. R., & Duncan, P. A.|year=1997|url=https://research.fs.usda.gov/treesearch/15626|title=Increase in distribution records of owl species in Manitoba based on a volunteer nocturnal survey using Boreal Owl (''Aegolius funereus'') and Great Gray Owl (''Strix nebulosa'') playback|editor1=Duncan, James R.|editor2=Johnson, David H.|editor3=Nicholls, Thomas H.|journal=Biology and Conservation of Owls of the Northern Hemisphere: 2nd International Symposium|publisher=Gen. Tech. Rep. NC-190. St. Paul, MN: US Dept. of Agriculture, Forest Service, North Central Forest Experiment Station|pages=519–524|volume=190}}</ref> The barred owl has been present in Manitoba at least since 1886, Alberta since 1932 and Saskatchewan since 1948.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Seton|first=E. E. T. |year=1886|title=The birds of western Manitoba|journal=Auk |volume=3|issue=3 |pages=145–156, 320–329|doi=10.2307/4625404
|jstor=4625404 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/part/87645 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Preble|first=E. A. |year=1941|title=Barred Owl on Athabaska River, Alberta|journal= The Auk |volume=58|issue=3 |pages=407–408|doi=10.2307/4078969 |jstor=4078969 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.29173/bluejay2494 |title=First Saskatchewan Nest Record of Barred Owl |date=1961 |last=Houston |first=Stuart |journal=Blue Jay |volume=19 |issue=3 |doi-access=free }}</ref> However, a study in Alberta has shown that barred owls have likely been present for no less than 100 years.<ref>{{cite journal|title=The Barred Owl, ''Strix varia'' in Alberta: distribution and status|date=2004 |doi=10.22621/cfn.v118i2.916 |last=Priestley |first=Lisa Takats |journal=The Canadian Field-Naturalist |volume=118 |issue=2 |page=215 |doi-access=free }}</ref> This owl species currently ranges through much of [[British Columbia]], where they have been expanding their range since at least 1943, including [[Vancouver Island]] and as far north as [[Fort St. John, British Columbia|Fort St. John]].<ref name= Campbell>{{cite book|author=Campbell, W., Dawe, N. K., McTaggart-Cowan, I., Cooper, J. M., Kaiser, G. W., & McNall, M. C.|year=2011|title=Birds of British Columbia, Volume 2: Nonpasserines-Diurnal Birds of Prey through Woodpeckers|publisher=UBC Press}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/3536439|jstor=3536439 |title=The Barred Owl in British Columbia |last=Grant |first=James |journal=The Murrelet |date=1966 |volume=47 |issue=2 |pages=39–45 }}</ref> The barred owl has had a further northward expansion in the west to [[southeastern Alaska]] ([[Skagway, Alaska|Skagway]] to [[Ketchikan, Alaska|Ketchikan]]) and extreme southwestern [[Yukon]]. In the western United States they also range into northwestern [[Montana]] and northern [[Idaho]].<ref>{{cite book|author=Montana Bird Distribution Records Committee|year=1992|title=P. D. Skaar's Montana Bird Distribution|edition=4th|publisher=Montana Natural History Program|location=Helena, MT, USA}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Stephens, D. A., & Sturts, S. H.|year=1997|title=Idaho bird distribution: mapping by latilong|journal=Idaho Mus. Nat. Hist, Spec. Publ|issue=13}}</ref> Barred owls were first verified in southwest Montana in 1909 and in northwest Montana in 1921 (although reports of the species may date back to the 1870s) but not in Idaho until 1968.<ref name= Livezey2 /><ref name= Holt /> Of unknown origin, barred owls have been seen in [[Colorado]] since around the turn of the 20th century.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/4069130|jstor=4069130 |last1=Nehrling |first1=H. |title=Bird Notes from Sao Paulo, Brazil |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_auk_1900-07_17_3/page/298|journal=The Auk |date=1900 |volume=17 |issue=3 |pages=298–299 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/4071272|jstor=4071272 |last1=Cooke |first1=Wells W. |title=The Birds of Colorado: Third Supplement |journal=The Auk |date=1909 |volume=26 |issue=4 |pages=400–422 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/part/87307 }}</ref> They range broadly in eastern and western [[Washington (state)|Washington]] (reached by 1965) and western [[Oregon]] (reached by 1972) (mostly along the forested corridors hugging the [[montane]] areas of the [[Cascade Range|Cascade]], [[Olympic Mountains|Olympic]] and [[Blue Mountains (Pacific Northwest)|Blue]] ranges), and [[northern California]] (reached by 1976), now down to the [[Redwood National and State Parks|Redwood National Forest]], the [[Sierra Nevada (U.S.)|Sierra Nevada]] and outer [[San Francisco]].<ref>{{cite journal|author=Smith, M. R., Mattocks Jr., P.W. & Cassidy, K.M.|year=1997|title=Breeding birds of Washington State|journal=Washington State Gap Analysis – Final Report|editor1=K. M. Cassidy|editor2=C. E. Grue|editor3=M. R. Smith|editor4=K. M. Dvornich|publisher=Seattle Audubon Society Publications in Zoology no. 1.|location=Seattle, WA, USA}}</ref>

=== Habitat ===
[[File:Strix varia Barred Owl 3.8.2008.jpg|thumb|left|The barred owl usually makes its home in deciduous forests]]

The habitat used by barred owl is largely old [[Temperate deciduous forest|deciduous]], [[Temperate broadleaf and mixed forest|mixed forests]] and, occasionally, [[Temperate coniferous forest|coniferous forests]].<ref name= Konig /> [[Old growth forest]]s are preferred due to more extensive potential nest sites, less lower-branch density to impede hunting (and perhaps superior structural complexity to aid hunting), greater security from mobbing and perhaps greater [[thermoregulation]].<ref name= Nicholls>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/3799054|jstor=3799054 |title=Barred Owl Habitat Use as Determined by Radiotelemetry |last1=Nicholls |first1=Thomas H. |last2=Warner |first2=Dwain W. |journal=The Journal of Wildlife Management |date=1972 |volume=36 |issue=2 |pages=213–224 |url=https://digital.library.unt.edu/ark:/67531/metadc1033926/ |doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name= McGarigal2>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/3801804|jstor=3801804 |title=The Effect of Forest Stand Age on Owl Distribution in Southwestern Virginia |last1=McGarigal |first1=Kevin |last2=Fraser |first2=James D. |journal=The Journal of Wildlife Management |date=1984 |volume=48 |issue=4 |pages=1393–1398 }}</ref> They are often found in [[bottomland hardwood forest]]s in the largest swath of the native breeding range, often (particularly from [[Virginia]] south and west) with deep, dark stands of [[oak]], [[Nyssa sylvatica|gum]] and [[cypress]].<ref name= Voous /><ref name= Dickson>{{cite journal|author=Dickson, J. G. |year=1988|title=Bird communities in oak-gum-cypress forests|journal= Bird Conservation|volume= 3|pages= 51–62}}</ref> Secondary habitat, often used during foraging forays in the south, are often [[oak savanna]] or [[Sabal palmetto|cabbage-palm]] areas.<ref name= Voous /> Regardless of area, some variety of water is frequently present, including [[Riparian zone|riparian areas]] or [[swamp]]y ground.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Bent /><ref name= Laidig>{{cite journal|url=https://biostor.org/reference/215761|author1=Laidig, K. J.|author2=Dobkin, D. S. |year=1995|title=Spatial overlap and habitat associations of barred owls and great horned owls in southern New Jersey|journal= Journal of Raptor Research|volume= 29|issue=3|pages=151–157}}</ref> Closed canopy forests were preferred in a study from [[Oklahoma]] (62.8% of habitat used), followed by [[fallow]] [[field (agriculture)|agricultural fields]] (10.6%), [[wetland]]s (8.1%) and [[open terrain]] (6.2%). The latter three were visited during hunting forays, and wetlands and open terrain areas were not used outside of the breeding season.<ref name= Winton>{{cite journal|author1=Winton, B. R.|author2= Leslie, D. M. |year=2004|title=Density and habitat associations of barred owls at the edge of their range in Oklahoma|jstor=3878075|journal= Southeastern Naturalist|volume= 3|issue=3|pages= 475–482|doi= 10.1656/1528-7092(2004)003[0475:DAHAOB]2.0.CO;2 |s2cid= 85726566 }}</ref> Along the [[East Coast of the United States|Atlantic coast]] area from [[New England]] to [[New Jersey]], barred owls are often found in mixed swamps areas with [[cedrus|cedars]], seldom wandering to adjacent [[wetland]]s or [[Agriculture|farmland]].<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Sutton, C. C.|author2=Sutton, P. T. |year=1986|title=The status and distribution of Barred Owl and Red-shouldered Hawk in southern New Jersey|journal= Cassinia|volume= 61|pages= 20–29|url=https://dvoc.org/CassiniaOnLine/Cassinia61/C61_20_29.pdf}}</ref><ref>Smith, C. F. (1978). ''Distributional ecology of Barred and Great Horned owls in relation to human distribution''. Master's Thesis, Univ. of Connecticut, Hartford.</ref> The species may be at home in wooded areas in [[Montane ecosystems|mountainous regions]].<ref name= Konig /> More upland wooded habitats, often in mixed woods containing [[Tsuga|hemlock]], [[alder]], [[Populus|poplar]], [[pine]] and [[oak]], are typical in the northern part of the range.<ref name= Hume /><ref name= Laidig /> A study in northern New Jersey found at least 15 species of both conifer and deciduous trees were routinely used for differing purposes.<ref>Rothauser, B. (2003). [https://www.nj.gov/dep/fgw/ensp/pdf/end-thrtened/barredowl.pdf ''Barred Owl, Strix varia'']. NJ Fish and Wildlife.</ref> In Michigan, barred owl habitat usually consists largely of some combination of hemlock and [[maple]] trees, with mixed forest usage being use disproportionately to its prevalence in the environment.<ref name= Elody>{{cite journal|author1=Elody, B. I.|author2= Sloan, N. F. |year=1985|title=Movements and habitat use of Barred Owls in the Huron Mountains of Marquette County, Michigan, as determined by radiotelemetry|journal= Jack-Pine Warbler|volume= 63|issue=1|pages= 3–8}}</ref> Large [[oak]] stands were preferred in [[Minnesota]], a bit ahead of mixed forest and far ahead of [[Thuja occidentalis|white cedar]] swamps and other habitat types, which were either too dense, too open or had too few attractive nesting sites.<ref name= Nicholls /><ref name= Fuller>Fuller, M. R. (1979). ''Spatiotemporal ecology of four sympatric raptor species''. Phd Thesis, Univ. of Minnesota, Minneapolis.</ref>

Barred owls are not confined to extensive forest, also dwelling extensively in semi-open [[Woodland|wooded areas]], locally in [[Park|large parks]] with mature trees, and in [[Habitat fragmentation|forest adjacent regions]] recently [[Logging|logged]].<ref name= Konig /> Recent studies show [[suburb]]an neighborhoods can be ideal habitat for barred owls, and the species may be considered a local [[synanthrope]].<ref name= Bierregaard>Bierregaard, R. O. (2018). "Barred Owls: a nocturnal generalist thrives in wooded, suburban habitats", pp. 138–151 in ''Urban Raptors''. Island Press, Washington, DC. {{ISBN|9781610918398}}</ref> Using transmitters, scientists found that some regional populations, such as in [[Charlotte, North Carolina]], increased faster in the [[suburb]]an settings than in [[old growth forest]]. A factor of this suburban success may be easily accessible [[rodent]] prey in such settings. However, for breeding and roosting needs, this species needs at least some large trees and can be locally absent in some urban areas for this reason. The increased offspring offset the death rate due to impacts from cars, other types of collisions and disease.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.livescience.com/animals/071017-urban-owls.html|title=Owls Get Wise to Better Life in Cities|first=Jeanna|last=Bryner|website=LiveScience|year=2007}}</ref> Similarly, in [[Piedmont, South Carolina]], productivity of the owls was higher in suburban areas and they comprised 41% of the territories of the local owls but various sorts of [[Human impact on the environment|anthropogenic]] mortality were seemingly higher.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.5751/ACE-01427-140212 |title=Key habitat features facilitate the presence of Barred Owls in developed landscapes |date=2019 |last1=Clement |first1=Marion A. |last2=Barrett |first2=Kyle |last3=Baldwin |first3=Robert F. |journal=Avian Conservation and Ecology |volume=14 |issue=2 |s2cid=202860589 |doi-access=free }}</ref> In suburban areas of [[Ohio]], 41.4% of barred owl range was forested, 29.8% was low-density residential areas and less than 15% was [[pasture]].<ref name="Dykstra">{{cite journal|doi=10.3356/JRR-11-05.1|title=Habitats of Suburban Barred Owls (''Strix varia'') and Red-Shouldered Hawks (''Buteo lineatus'') in Southwestern Ohio|date=2012 |last1=Dykstra |first1=Cheryl R. |last2=Simon |first2=Melinda M. |last3=Daniel |first3=F. Bernard |last4=Hays |first4=Jeffrey L. |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |volume=46 |issue=2 |pages=190–200 |s2cid=86775451 |doi-access=free }}</ref> On the other hand, studies from the [[Northeastern United States]], such as in [[New Jersey]], found barred owls breeding mainly in plots of old-growth woodlands, and rarely successfully breeding in [[Peri-urbanisation|peri-urban areas]], in part because of competitive and predatory displacement by [[great horned owl]]s.<ref name= Laidig /><ref name= Bosakowski>Bosakowski, T., Speiser, R., & Benzinger, J. (1987). ''Distribution, density, and habitat relationships of the Barred Owl in northern New Jersey''. Biology and Conservation of Northern Owls. Gen. Tech. Re, RM-142. US Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Rocky Mountain Forest and Range Experiment Station, Fort Collins, 135–143.</ref> Furthermore, a study in [[North Carolina]] showed most barred owls appear to favor areas with at least {{convert|86|to|370|ha|acre|abbr=on}} of woods but did not seem to be affected by the presence of roadways.<ref>Rubino, M. J. (2001). ''Identifying barred owl habitat in the North Carolina Piedmont: using GIS in focal species conservation planning'' (Master's thesis, North Carolina State University.).</ref>

In the [[Pacific Northwest]], they can be quite adaptive to [[secondary forest]]s.<ref name= BNA /> On the other hand, in [[Ontario]] where the barred owl is native, secondary forest seemed to be largely avoided per a study.<ref>Van Ael, S. M. (1996). ''Modelling Barred Owl habitat in northwestern Ontario''. Master's Thesis, Lakehead Univ., Thunder Bay, ON.</ref> In the recent western part of the range, barred owls often dwell in mixed wood areas, often where there are lowland stands of [[Populus balsamifera|balsam poplar]], [[Populus tremuloides|trembling aspen]] and [[Picea glauca|white spruce]], occasionally but not commonly in pure [[Pinophyta|conifer]] [[Boreal forest|boreal stands]].<ref name= Mazur2>Mazur, K. M., James, P. C., Fitzsimmons, M. J., Langen, G., & Espie, R. H. M. (1997). ''Habitat associations of the Barred Owl in the boreal forest of Saskatchewan, Canada''. Journal of Raptor Research, 31(3), 253–259.</ref><ref name= Takats>Takats, D. L. (1998). ''Barred Owl habitat use and distribution in the Foothills Model Forest''. Master's Thesis, Univ. of Alberta, Edmonton.</ref><ref name= Dunbar>{{cite journal|author1=Dunbar, D. L.|year=1991|title=Status of the spotted owl, ''Strix occidentalis'', and barred owl, ''Strix varia'', in southwestern British Columbia|journal=Canadian Field-Naturalist|volume=105|issue=4|pages= 464–468|doi=10.5962/p.358095 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Barred owls in California preferred stands of [[Alnus rubra|red alder]]. More so confined to inland areas, as in eastern Washington, Idaho, Manitoba and Montana, they prefer Douglas fir, [[Pinus ponderosa|ponderosa pine]], [[Betula papyrifera|paper birch]], [[Quercus macrocarpa|burr oak]] and [[Larix occidentalis|western larch]]. In north-central Alberta, the use of old growth forest was far more prevalent than its occurrence in the wild.<ref name= Olsen>Olsen, B. (1999). ''Breeding habitat ecology of the barred owl (Strix varia) at three spatial scales in the boreal mixedwood forest of north-central Alberta''. UNM. {{doi|10.7939/R3280575V}}</ref> In the [[Cascades Range]] of Washington, barred owls usually dwell in areas with more [[Abies grandis|grand firs]], taller and more diverse tree heights, more enclosed canopies, higher numbers of trees per acre and less ground cover.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.3356/rapt-49-02-129-140.1|author=Singleton, P. H. |title=Forest structure within barred owl (''Strix varia'') home ranges in the Eastern Cascade Range, Washington|journal=Journal of Raptor Research |date=2015 |volume=49 |issue=2 |pages=129–140 |s2cid=83958216 |doi-access=free }}</ref> In Saskatchewan, barred owls preferred areas with a minimum of 66% forest cover.<ref name= Grossman>{{cite journal|title=Responses of Great Horned Owls (''Bubo virginianus''), Barred Owls (''Strix varia''), and Northern Saw-whet Owls (''Aegolius acadicus'') to forest cover and configuration in an agricultural landscape in Alberta, Canada|date=2008 |doi=10.1139/Z08-095 |last1=Grossman |first1=S. R. |last2=Hannon |first2=S. J. |last3=Sánchez-Azofeifa |first3=A. |journal=Canadian Journal of Zoology |volume=86 |issue=10 |pages=1165–1172 }}</ref>

== Behavior ==
[[File:Barred Owl (Canada).jpg|thumb|Barred pattern, Ontario, Canada]]

The barred owl, like most owls, is largely adapted to [[nocturnality]].<ref name= Konig /> Between 5:00{{nbsp}}am and 8:00{{nbsp}} pm, juvenile barred owls were recorded to sleep an average of 28% of each hour.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Hamerstrom, F.|author2=Janick, K. |year=1973|title=Diurnal sleep rhythm of a young barred owl|journal=The Auk|volume= 90|issue=4|pages= 899–900|doi=10.2307/4084377 |jstor=4084377 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Peak times in [[Minnesota]] were found to be right after sunset and just before dawn.<ref name= Tester>{{cite journal|author1=Tester, J. R. |year=1987|url=https://www.cedarcreek.umn.edu/biblio/fulltext/t1153.pdf|title=Changes in daily activity rhythms of some free-ranging animals in Minnesota|journal=Canadian Field-Naturalist|volume= 101|issue=1|pages=13–21|doi=10.5962/p.355849 }}</ref> Nonetheless, they are not as fully nocturnal as many owls and rank around 6th amongst 19 regular North American owl species for the regularity of their activity outside of nightfall, especially in particular circumstances such as when a rival or a human impersonator is emitting barred owls calls or whilst hunting. Often daytime activity tends to be early in the morning or around dusk but potentially at any time ([[overcast]] days being preferred).<ref name= Caldwell>{{cite journal|author1=Caldwell, L. D. |year=1972|title=Diurnal hunting by a Barred Owl|journal=Jack Pine Warbler |volume= 50|pages=93–94}}</ref><ref name= Jackson>{{cite journal|author1=Jackson, J. A.|author2= White, R. |year=1995|title=Diurnal roadside hunting by Barred Owls|journal= Journal of Louisiana Ornithology|volume= 3|pages= 13–15}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Campbell, J. M. |year=1969|title=The owls of the central Brooks Range, Alaska|journal=The Auk|volume= 86|issue=3|pages= 565–568|doi=10.2307/4083428 |jstor=4083428 }}</ref> This species often spends the daytime hidden away in the dense foliage of a tree, often at minimum {{convert|5|m|ft|abbr=on}} above the ground, but sometimes also roosts in a branch close to a broad trunk or in a natural tree hollow.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Voous /> Roost tree heights in [[Minnesota]] was typically {{convert|8|to|12|m|ft|abbr=on}} while, in [[Illinois]], they were up to {{convert|9|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Blakemore>{{cite journal|author1=Blakemore, L. A. |year=1940|title=Barred Owl food habits in Glenwood Park, Minneapolis, Minnesota|journal= Flicker|volume= 12|pages= 21–23}}</ref><ref name= Applegate>{{cite journal|author1=Applegate, R. D. |year=1975|title=Co-roosting of Barred Owls and Common Grackles|journal= Bird-Banding |volume=46|issue=2 |pages= 169–170|doi=10.2307/4512120 |jstor=4512120 }}</ref> Recently fledged owls sometimes roost in tall grass, usually after falling from the nest tree.<ref name= Dunstan />

Roost site selection may be partially dictated by [[thermoregulation]], as in spotted owls, with shadier roosts likely to mitigate [[Hyperthermia|heat stress]].<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Barrows, C. W. |year=1981|title=Roost selection by Spotted Owls: an adaptation to heat stress|journal= Condor|volume= 83|issue=4 |pages= 302–309|doi=10.2307/1367496 |jstor=1367496 }}</ref> They seldom rely on [[camouflage]], instead often flying at the least disturbance and not allowing close approaches, making them potentially difficult to observe.<ref name= Hume /> Yet, on the other hand, they can be surprisingly tame and seemingly curious of people in the wild; further they are considered "as mild and engaging" as a predator can be.<ref name= Hume /> Barred owls are regularly subject to [[Mobbing (animal behavior)|mobbing]] by small birds, from several small [[passerine]]s to [[corvid]]s and [[woodpecker]]s, and mammals when discovered by them during the daytime, and such situations may lead to them being attacked by diurnal birds of prey.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Bent /><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Carter, J. D. |year=1925|title=Behavior of the Barred Owl|journal=The Auk|volume=42|issue=3 |pages=443–444|doi=10.2307/4074397 |jstor=4074397 |doi-access=free}}</ref> There are some records of barred owls engaging in [[Preening (bird)|allopreening]], presumably between pairs, with each other in the wild.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Fitzpatrick, J. W. |year=1975|title=A record of allopreening in the Barred Owl|journal=The Auk|volume=92|issue=3 |pages= 598–599|doi=10.2307/4084622 |jstor=4084622 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Scott, D. |year=1980|title=Owls allopreening in the wild|journal= British Birds|volume= 73|pages= 436–39}}</ref> They are skilled and silent fliers and frequently use routine forest flyways with open understory and low branch densities.<ref name= Nicholls/>

[[File:Strix varia DM1.jpg|thumb|left|The barred owl is normally nocturnally active and sleeps during the day]]

Like most species of owl in the ''Strix'' genus, the barred owl tends to be highly [[Territory (animal)|territorial]] regardless of the time of year.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= McGarigal2 /><ref name= Mosher /><ref name= Nicholls /> The territories are claimed by singing from different perches, often near the perimeter of its perceived home range. The boundaries are almost always well-maintained by barred owls and are generally stable from year to year and even generation to generation.<ref name= Nicholls /> Territory sizes have been determined via [[Wildlife radio telemetry|radio telemetry]].<ref name= Konig /> The average territory size of 13 in Minnesota was {{convert|273|ha|acre|abbr=on}}, of 7 in Michigan it was {{convert|282|ha|acre|abbr=on}} and of 10 in Wisconsin was {{convert|337.9|ha|acre|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Olsen /><ref name= Nicholls /> Another Minnesota study found pairs to occupy a mean of about {{convert|226|ha|acre|abbr=on}} in mixed hardwood-conifer woods.<ref name= Konig /> Pairs in an Oklahoma study were reportedly found every {{convert|110|to|165|ha|acre|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Winton /> The mean territory size from 10 studies was estimated from throughout the range. In this study, the breeding season mean area was calculated at {{convert|256.7|ha|acre|abbr=on}} in males and {{convert|297.8|ha|acre|abbr=on}} in females; whereas the means in nonbreeding season were {{convert|900.4|ha|acre|abbr=on}} in males and {{convert|536.2|ha|acre|abbr=on}} in females. Overall, the annual mean home range for males was {{convert|782|ha|acre|abbr=on}} and for females was {{convert|538.7|ha|acre|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Livezey5 /> The breeding range's mean home size in Washington was {{convert|321|ha|acre|abbr=on}}, while it was {{convert|971|ha|acre|abbr=on}} in the non-breeding season.

[[File:Barred owl bathing.webm|thumb|right|Barred owl bathing in Brett Woods, [[Fairfield, Connecticut]]]]
The ranges of pairs overlaps in the breeding season, at 87–95% range overlap, but decreases down to 45% after hatching.<ref name="Fuller"/> In a Florida study of barred owls, territorial responses, including several [[duet]]s, by a pair were found to be provoked by researchers playing calls of both "stranger" owls and recordings of owls that were neighbors to the pair being tested. The aggressive response even to known neighbors in this study is unusual.<ref name="Mennill">{{cite journal |last1=Mennill |first1=Daniel |last2=Odom |first2=Karan |date=2010 |title=Vocal duets in a nonpasserine: An examination of territory defence and neighbour–stranger discrimination in a neighbourhood of barred owls |journal=Behaviour |volume=147 |issue=5–6 |pages=619–639 |doi=10.1163/000579510X12632972452424}}</ref> In [[tawny owl]]s in a study from [[Italy]], for instance, they responded mildly or not at all to the calls of neighboring tawny owls known to them but with great aggression to the calls of "stranger" owls.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1993.tb02847.x|title=Differential responses of territorial Tawny Owls ''Strix aluco'' to the hooting of neighbours and strangers|date=2008 |last1=Galeotti |first1=Paolo |last2=Pavan |first2=Gianni |journal=Ibis |volume=135 |issue=3 |pages=300–304 }}</ref> Due to its rather stolidly territorial nature, the barred owl is not normally a migratory species.<ref name= Konig /> Claims in the past of "rather impressive" flights in [[New England]] in the past were lacking in verifiable details.<ref name= Voous /> Of 158 banded recoveries in the northern part of the range, movements during winter were found to cover no further than {{convert|10|km|mi|abbr=on}}, while all those recovered in Saskatchewan and Alberta scarcely moved at all.<ref name= Johnsgard /><ref name= Campbell /><ref name= Mazur3>Mazur, K. M. (1997). [https://www.nlc-bnc.ca/obj/s4/f2/dsk3/ftp04/mq30518.pdf ''Spatial habitat selection by Barred Owls in the boreal forest of Saskatchewan, Canada'']. Master's Thesis, Univ. of Regina, Regina, SK.</ref> There is little to no evidence of nomadic behavior as has been recorded in several other owls in the north.<ref name= Nicholls2>Nicholls, T. H. & Fuller, M. R. (1987). ''Territorial Aspects of Barred Owl Home Range and Behavior in Minnesota''. In ''Biology and Conservation of Northern Forest Owls: Symposium Proceedings''. February 3–7, 1987, Winnipeg, Manitoba (Vol. 142, p. 121). Fort Collins, Colo.: Rocky Mountain Forest and Range Experiment Station.</ref><ref name= Elody2>Elody, B. I. (1983). ''Techniques for capturing, marking, monitoring, and habitat analysis for the Barred Owl in the Upper Peninsula of Michigan''. Master's Thesis, Michigan Technological Univ., Houghton.</ref>

== Dietary biology ==
[[File:Barred 2010 3 web.jpg|thumb|left|Small mammals tend to be the characteristic prey for barred owls]]

Barred owls are opportunistic predators of the woodlands. Like the tawny owl, the barred owl usually hunts from a perch. During hunting efforts, they glide briefly from perch to perch until prey is detected.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Hume /> The barred owl has incredibly large eyes that capture as much light as possible, allowing for better night vision. Attacks may be carried out merely {{convert|6|to|10|m|ft|abbr=on}} away from the prey due to the effectiveness of the silencing wing feathers.<ref>Lilley, G. (1998).  ''A study of the silent flight of the owl''. In 4th AIAA/CEAS aeroacoustics conference (p. 2340).</ref> The barred owl, especially compared to the predominantly [[Arboreal locomotion|arboreal]] prey of the spotted owl, usually prefers to target small animals that are [[Terrestrial animal|terrestrial]].<ref name= Wiens>{{cite journal|doi=10.1002/wmon.1009 |title=Competitive interactions and resource partitioning between northern spotted owls and barred owls in western Oregon |date=2014 |last1=Wiens |first1=J. David |last2=Anthony |first2=Robert G. |last3=Forsman |first3=Eric D. |journal=Wildlife Monographs |volume=185 |issue=1 |pages=1–50 |bibcode=2014WildM.185....1W }}</ref> However, barred owls will also flush and capture night-roosting birds, and capture bats on the wing as well.<ref name= Konig /> Daytime hunting has been reported several times, although peak hunting time is typically shortly after sunset.<ref name= Bent /><ref name= Caldwell /><ref name= Jackson /><ref name= Sutherland>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/138641|author1=Sutherland, D. A.|author2= Jones, I. L. |year=2010|title=Successful Diurnal Foraging by a Barred Owl|journal=Ontario Birds|volume=28|issue=3|pages=152–155}}</ref> Although they usually hunt within woodlands, they also occasionally hunt in [[open terrain]], more typical of the hunting areas of a bird like the long-eared owl.<ref name= Hume /> Cases of snow-plunging have been verified for barred owls, allowing them to capture prey like [[vole]]s in [[Subnivean climate|subnivean zones]] that they use as hidden [[snow]] tunnels during winter, a hunting method once thought particular to [[great grey owl]]s.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.29173/bluejay5428 |title=Evidence of Snow-Plunging by Boreal and Barred Owls |date=1993 |last1=Nero |first1=Robert W. |journal=Blue Jay |volume=51 |issue=3 |s2cid=221969147 |doi-access=free }}</ref> While hunting [[American red squirrel|squirrels]] in the [[Alberta–British Columbia foothills forests|Foothill Model Forest]] of [[Alberta]], barred owls were seen to make several passes before succeeding.<ref name= Takats2>{{cite journal|author=Takats, D. L. |year=1996|title=Foraging observations of a Barred Owl in the Foothills Model Forest|journal= Alberta Naturalist |volume=26|pages= 29–30}}</ref> Hunting on the ground is usually done to obtain foods such as [[invertebrate]]s or [[amphibian]]s.<ref name= Dunstan /><ref name= Elderkin>Elderkin, M. F. (1987). ''The breeding and feeding ecology of a Barred Owl Strix varia Barton population in Kings County, Nova Scotia''. Master's Thesis, Acadia Univ., Wolfville, NS.</ref> These owls may wade into shallow water to capture fish and may do an unusual amount of aquatic foraging, as compared to any ''Strix'' species or North American owl.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Hume />

Due to its relatively modest foot size, it does not usually take particularly large prey.<ref name= Hume /> However, owls in general have proportionately larger feet and more powerful [[Flexor digitorum longus muscle|grips]] than similarly sized diurnal raptors, while the physiology of the [[Accipitridae|daytime raptors]] [[Flexor hallucis longus muscle|differs]]. While the mechanism of the killing feet overlap, owls kill mainly with [[constriction]] and sacrifice [[velocity]] with their physiology while diurnal raptors have higher velocity and kill mainly by [[Major trauma|trauma]] inflicted by their enlarged talons.<ref name= Ward>{{cite journal|doi=10.1093/auk/119.4.1052 |title=Functional Morphology of Raptor Hindlimbs: Implications for Resource Partitioning |date=2002 |last1=Ward |first1=Andrea B. |last2=Weigl |first2=Peter D. |last3=Conroy |first3=Rachael M. |journal=The Auk |volume=119 |issue=4 |pages=1052–1063 |doi-access=free }}</ref> A majority of prey of barred owls is eaten outright but, with large prey, the barred owl may eat the head first and then return to consume the remainder of the body.<ref name= Elderkin /> Cases of owls of any variety [[Scavenger|scavenging]] on [[carrion]] are generally rare, but at least three instances of carrion-feeding by barred owls have been observed, more recently eating [[White-tailed deer|deer]] and [[Eastern gray squirrel|squirrel]] [[roadkill]]s on a remote camera in North Carolina.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Carrion-feeding by Barred Owls (''Strix varia'')|jstor=23033577 |last1=Kapfer |first1=Joshua M. |last2=Gammon |first2=David E. |last3=Groves |first3=John D. |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |date=2011 |volume=123 |issue=3 |pages=646–649 |doi=10.1676/11-015.1 |s2cid=85768266 }}</ref><ref>Forbush, E. H. (1925). ''Birds of Massachusetts and other New England states. Volume 2''. Massachusetts Department of Agriculture, Boston, US.</ref> The [[Pellet (ornithology)|pellet]] of the barred owl averages about {{convert|3.5|cm|in|abbr=on}} in diameter and {{convert|7.2|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length.<ref name= BNA />

[[File:Bird lore (1908) (14750520262).jpg|thumb|A 1908 photo of a site found with a massive amount of barred owl casts and pellets near [[Carlisle, Pennsylvania]]]]

The barred owl has been known to consume a diversity of animals from different [[Class (taxonomy)|taxonomic classes]]. Primarily, these owls live off of small [[mammal]]s.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Mikkola /> Other [[vertebrate]]s are rarely neglected though, especially [[bird]]s and [[amphibian]]s, but also occasionally [[reptile]]s and [[fish]]. For an owl its size, the barred owl also consumes a large amount of [[arthropod]]s and other [[invertebrate]]s.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Livezey5>{{cite journal|author=Livezey, K.B.|year= 2007|title=Barred Owl habitat and prey: a review and synthesis of the literature |journal= Journal of Raptor Research |doi=10.3356/0892-1016(2007)41[177:BOHAPA]2.0.CO;2|volume=41|issue= 3|pages=177–201|s2cid= 86266928|url= https://zenodo.org/record/1236455|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name= Worm>{{cite journal |author=Livezey, K.B. |author2=Elderkin, M.F. |author3=Cott, P.A. |author4=Hobbs, J. |author5=Hudson, J.P. |year= 2008|title= Barred owls eating worms and slugs: the advantage in not being picky eaters|jstor=30135134|journal= Northwestern Naturalist |doi=10.1898/NWN08-04.1|volume=89|issue= 3|pages=185–190|s2cid= 86062164}}</ref><ref name="fcps.edu">[http://www.fcps.edu/islandcreekes/ecology/barred_owl.htm ''Barred Owl''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171027093706/http://www.fcps.edu/islandcreekes/ecology/barred_owl.htm |date=2017-10-27 }}. Fcps.edu. Retrieved on 2016-08-01.</ref> One study from a wide swath of the range found that among 2234 accrued prey items, 76% were mammals, 15.8% were invertebrates, 5.8% were birds and 2.5% were other vertebrates.<ref name= Snyder>Snyder, N. F., & Wiley, J. W. (1976). ''Sexual size dimorphism in hawks and owls of North America (No. 20)''. American Ornithologists' Union.</ref> In four other studies from different parts of the distribution, the mean balance of mammals in diet was around 64.9%, birds at around 13.4%, invertebrates at around 11.4% and different classes of vertebrates (mostly amphibians) at around 10.3%.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Takats /><ref name= Elderkin /><ref name= Marks>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/3534209|jstor=3534209 |title=Winter Food Habits of Barred Owls in Western Montana |last1=Marks |first1=Jeffrey S. |last2=Hendricks |first2=D. Paul |last3=Marks |first3=Victoria S. |journal=The Murrelet |date=1984 |volume=65 |issue=1 |pages=27–28 }}</ref><ref name= Bosakowski2>{{cite journal|doi=10.1139/z92-140 |title=Comparative diets of sympatric nesting raptors in the eastern deciduous forest biome |date=1992 |last1=Bosakowski |first1=Thomas |last2=Smith |first2=Dwight G. |journal=Canadian Journal of Zoology |volume=70 |issue=5 |pages=984–992 }}</ref> A compilation study that included a total of 7077 prey items using all methodologies, 71.9% were mammals, 9.5% were birds, 0.6% reptiles, 6% amphibians, 1.89% fish, 1% [[earthworm]]s, 0.2% [[gastropod]]s, 6.5% [[insect]]s and 2.4% [[crayfish]].<ref name= Livezey5 /> Barred owls tend to focus on fairly small-sized prey, although are capable of attacking larger than usual prey in infrequent cases.<ref name= Wiens /><ref name= Livezey5 /> The mean size of prey taken is seldom estimated in the barred owls' eastern range, although one study estimated mean size of prey in the general east was {{convert|33.5|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Marti>{{cite book|doi=10.1007/978-1-4615-9582-3_2|isbn=978-1-4615-9582-3 |chapter=Trophic Structure of Raptor Communities: A Three-Continent Comparison and Synthesis |title=Current Ornithology |date=1993 |last1=Marti |first1=Carl D. |last2=Korpimäki |first2=Erkki |last3=Jaksić |first3=Fabian M. |pages=47–137 }}</ref> Many more studies have estimated mean prey masses in westerly areas of sympatry with spotted owls to understand how their diets may conflict.<ref name= Wiens /> Different studies from the west (mainly [[Washington (state)|Washington]] and [[Oregon]]) have variously estimated the mean prey sizes for barred owls at {{convert|47.7|g|oz|abbr=on}}, {{convert|56.1|g|oz|abbr=on}}, {{convert|60.2|g|oz|abbr=on}}, {{convert|103.5|g|oz|abbr=on}} and {{convert|123.6|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Wiens /><ref name= Marti /><ref name= Graham>Graham, S. A. (2012). [https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/61724451.pdf ''Diet composition, niche and geographic characteristics, and prey size preference of Barred Owls (Strix varia) in the Pacific Northwest'']. Masters Thesis, Oregon State University.</ref><ref name= Hamer>{{cite journal |author=Hamer, T.E. |author2=Hays, D.L. |author3=Senger, C.M. |author4=Forsman, E.D. |year= 2001|title= Diets of northern barred owls and northern spotted owls in an area of sympatry|journal= Journal of Raptor Research |url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/53893|volume=35|pages=221–227}}</ref>

=== Mammals ===
The predominant small mammals available in forest and woodland edges are generally small [[rodent]]s, so the barred owl, like other ''Strix'' owls, most often relies on rodents as the primary type of food.<ref name="Livezey5" /> Preferred rodents to be taken are [[vole]]s, [[Mouse|mice]] of the genus ''[[Peromyscus]]'' and assorted [[rat]]s, including non-native ''[[Rattus]]'' species as well as unrelated native types like [[cotton rat]]s, [[Oryzomys|rice rats]] and [[Pack rat|woodrats]]. These all share with barred owls a penchant for [[nocturnality]] and [[Crepuscular animal|crepuscular]] habits (although many voles are more correctly considered [[Cathemerality|cathemeral]])<ref name="Konig" /><ref name="Voous" /><ref name="Johnsgard" /> While during other seasons, the diet of barred owls can be fairly diverse, the winter diet may be almost wholly rodents.<ref name="Livezey5" /> This was the case in winter in [[Montana]], where 97.6% of 1153 prey items were [[montane vole]]s or [[meadow vole]]s, with a possible slight mixture of other voles.<ref name= Holt2>{{cite journal|doi=10.1898/1051-1733(2007)88[7:BOWDAP]2.0.CO;2|issn=1051-1733 |date=2007 |volume=88 |page=7 |title=Barred Owl Winter Diet and Pellet Dimensions in Western Montana |last1=Holt |first1=Denver W. |last2=Bitter |first2=Colleen |journal=Northwestern Naturalist |s2cid=86350479 }}</ref> The diet of barred owls in a much smaller study near [[Urbana, Illinois]] during winter was less homogeneous but still led by rodents, especially the meadow vole (32.3%) and [[white-footed mouse]] (23.5%).<ref name= Cahn>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/4075480|jstor=4075480 |last1=Cahn |first1=Alvin R. |last2=Kemp |first2=Jack T. |title=On the Food of Certain Owls in East-Central Illinois |journal=The Auk |date=1930 |volume=47 |issue=3 |pages=323–328 }}</ref> A winter food study in [[Essex County, New Jersey]] found that among 118 prey items, meadow voles comprised a great majority of the prey, at 91.5% of the balance.<ref name= Rusling>{{cite journal|author=Rusling, W. J. |year=1951|title=Food habits of New Jersey owls|journal=Proc. Linnaean Soc. New York|volume=58–62|pages=38–45}}</ref>

An unusual lack of diversity in barred owl pellets was found in several years of possibly an aseasonal study in [[Ann Arbor, Michigan]] where of 777 prey items, 83.3% were meadow voles.<ref name= Wilson>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/4078196|jstor=4078196 |last1=Wilson |first1=Kenneth A. |title=Owl Studies at Ann Arbor, Michigan |journal=The Auk |date=1938 |volume=55 |issue=2 |pages=187–197 |doi-access=free }}</ref> At Edwin S. George Preserve near the [[University of Michigan]], the summer diet was also heavily rodent based, as among 146 prey items 37.9% were white-footed mice, 22.6% were [[southern bog lemming]] and 6.84% were meadow voles.<ref name= Hamerstrom>{{cite journal|author1=Hamerstrom Jr, F. N.|author2= Hamerstrom, F. |year=1951|title=Food of young raptors on the Edwin S. George Reserve|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/127017 |journal=The Wilson Bulletin|pages= 16–25}}</ref> In a somewhat larger Michigan study, the [[North American deermouse]], lead the prey at 34.9% of 321 prey items.<ref name= Errington>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/1363563|jstor=1363563 |last1=Errington |first1=Paul L. |title=Food Habits of Southern Wisconsin Raptors. Part I. Owls |journal=The Condor |date=1932 |volume=34 |issue=4 |pages=176–186 }}</ref> In [[Minnesota]], the barred owl was counted as one of the leading causes of mortality of [[prairie vole]]s.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1093/jmammal/17.3.291|title=''Microtus ochrogaster'' in Minnesota |url=https://archive.org/details/sim_journal-of-mammalogy_1936-08-14_17_3/page/290|date=1936 |last1=Allan |first1=P. F. |journal=Journal of Mammalogy |volume=17 |issue=3 |page=291 }}</ref> Studies of the barred owl diet in 6 urban [[metropolitan area|metropolitan]] areas of [[British Columbia]] found that the diet was dominated by young rats of the invasive ''[[Rattus]]'' genus, comprising 52.8% of 688 prey items, well ahead of native [[Townsend's vole]]s, which were secondary at 19.2% of the diet.
