The average weight rats taken by owls were clearly juveniles, estimated to average {{convert|103|g|oz|abbr=on}}, although several could be anywhere from infant rat to adult rat sizes, i.e. about {{convert|25|to|300|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Hindmarch>{{cite journal|doi=10.3356/jrr-14-00012.1 |title=When Owls go to Town: The Diet of Urban Barred Owls |date=2015 |last1=Hindmarch |first1=Sofi |last2=Elliott |first2=John E. |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |volume=49 |pages=66–74 |s2cid=59379580 |doi-access=free }}</ref> The mean size of [[black rat]]s taken in Oregon was {{convert|250|g|oz|abbr=on}}, indicating that here large adults of this species were selected.<ref name= Graham /> Beyond the typical more meadow-dwelling voles and woodland edge-dwelling native mice, larger and more forest dwelling rodents of different varieties can be of variable import.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Hamer /> Numerous [[Pack rat|woodrat]] species may be taken and may provide a hearty meal to a barred owl, at a mean body mass when taken (in [[Oregon]]) of {{convert|285|g|oz|abbr=on}} for unidentified species.<ref name= Wiens /><ref name= Graham /><ref>{{cite journal|title=Observed Predation of ''Neotoma magister'' (Allegheny Woodrat) by ''Strix varia'' (Barred Owl) in Virginia|date=2020 |doi=10.1656/045.027.0103 |last1=Powers |first1=Karen E. |last2=Dimas |first2=Miranda S. |last3=Leon |first3=Angie I. |last4=Vanmeter |first4=Logan M. |journal=Northeastern Naturalist |volume=27 |s2cid=213605365 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=HaySmith, L. |year=1995|title=''Neotoma floridana floridana'': natural history, populations, and movements in northcentral Florida|journal= Bulletin of the Florida Museum of Natural History|volume= 38|pages= 211–243|doi=10.58782/flmnh.gdie1905 |url=https://www.floridamuseum.ufl.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/35/2017/03/Vol-38-No-8.pdf}}</ref>

In different areas, barred owls may regularly hunt the diverse members of the [[squirrel]] family, despite their general penchant for [[diurnality]]. Smaller squirrel varieties are usually focused on when hunted as supplement prey, such as [[chipmunk]]s, averaging about {{convert|83|g|oz|abbr=on}} among the different species they prey upon, and [[pine squirrel]]s, which average about twice as large as chipmunks.<ref name= Takats /><ref name= Wiens /><ref name= Graham /><ref name= Merritt>{{cite journal|url=http://ilacadofsci.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/12/106-12MS1305Final.pdf|author1=Meritt, D. A. Jr.|author2= Eul, R. |year=2013|title=Barred Owl pellet contents in Michigan|journal= Illinois State Academy of Science. Transactions|volume= 106|page= 55}}</ref> Usually juvenile specimens are focused on when taking the larger ''[[Sciurus]]'' [[tree squirrel]]s, at least in summer, but presumably a mixture of yearling and adult ''Sciurus'' will be taken during winter.<ref name= Wiens /> The mean weight of [[western gray squirrel]]s taken during the breeding season in Oregon was {{convert|450|g|oz|abbr=on}}, against a mean adult weight of around {{convert|770|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Graham /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hayssen |first1=Virginia |title=Patterns of Body and Tail Length and Body Mass in Sciuridae |journal=Journal of Mammalogy |date=15 August 2008 |volume=89 |issue=4 |pages=852–873 |doi=10.1644/07-MAMM-A-217.1|s2cid=29249283 |doi-access=free }}</ref> The issue of temporal activities is less pertinent to the predator of [[flying squirrel]]s, which are nocturnal. All studies of the diet of barred owls in [[Pacific Northwest]] show the importance of the [[northern flying squirrel]] to their diet. This flying squirrel was found to comprise from about 10.9% to 20% of the diet of barred owls (either as the most or second most important prey species) and, with a mean weight of {{convert|134|g|oz|abbr=on}} when taken, they comprised up to 25.6% of the food [[Biomass (ecology)|biomass]] for this owl species.<ref name= Wiens /><ref name= Graham /><ref name= Hamer /> In [[Green Ridge State Forest]] in [[Maryland]], although not numerically the most important prey family compared to unidentified cricetids and shrews, the [[southern flying squirrel]] was the most often identified prey species for barred owls.<ref name= Devereux>{{cite journal|author1=Devereux, J. G.|author2= Mosher, J. A. |year=1984|title=Breeding ecology of Barred Owls in the central Appalachians|journal= Raptor Research|volume= 18|issue=2|pages=49–58|url=https://biostor.org/reference/216646}}</ref> Beyond the aforementioned rodent prey, more infrequently rodent prey can including various other [[Cricetidae|cricetid rodents]], [[pocket gopher]]s, [[mountain beaver]]s (average weight when taken of up to {{convert|550|g|lb|abbr=on}}) and [[jumping mice]].<ref name= Wiens /><ref name= Blakemore /><ref name= Graham /> The largest known rodent prey of barred owls are adult [[muskrat]], which were estimated to weigh {{convert|1169|g|lb|abbr=on}} when taken.<ref name= Graham />

[[File:Barred owl (Strix varia) dining on a mole.webm|thumb|left|A barred owl consuming a mole (possibly a [[coast mole]]) in [[Washington (state)|Washington]]]]

The other primary mammalian prey types are the [[shrew]]s and the [[Mole (animal)|moles]]. At least a dozen species of shrew and most North American species of mole are known as prey of the barred owl.<ref name= Sutherland /><ref name= Livezey5 /><ref name= Wiens /><ref name= Graham /> 12.8% of 7077 total prey items from across the range were [[Eulipotyphla|shrews or moles]].<ref name= Livezey5 /> A small sample of prey in [[Michigan]] was led by the very small [[masked shrew]], which weighs around {{convert|4|g|oz|abbr=on}}, at 24% of 34 prey items.<ref name= Merritt /> A much larger shrew, the [[northern short-tailed shrew]] at around {{convert|21|g|oz|abbr=on}}, was the leading prey in [[Glenwood, Minnesota]] at 36% of 81 prey items.<ref name= Blakemore /> This prey species also is taken quite regularly in several other parts of the range, as well as a [[Southern short-tailed shrew|closely-related species]].<ref name= Devereux /> Assorted other shrew species and the smallest of the world's moles, the [[American shrew mole]] are regular supplement prey elsewhere, especially in the Pacific Northwest.<ref name= Wiens /><ref name= Graham /><ref name= Hamer /> The most frequently taken single prey species through the Pacific Northwest, at 11.8% of 4299 total prey items of barred owls, was the {{convert|56|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[coast mole]].<ref name="Wiens" /> Usually, moles are secondary if relatively hearty prey elsewhere in the range.<ref name="Whiklo2">Whiklo, T. (2012). ''Nest structure and breeding habitat characteristics of Barred Owls (Strix varia) in Manitoba, Canada''. {{hdl|1993/5218}} {{doi|10.13140/RG.2.2.32357.19682}}</ref>

Secondary prey can include several species of [[cottontail rabbit]]s (''Sylvilagus'' sp.) and [[snowshoe hare]]s (''Lepus americanus'').<ref name= Wiens /><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/3799058|jstor=3799058 |title=Twenty Years of Avian Predator-Small Mammal Relationships in Missouri |last1=Korschgen |first1=Leroy J. |last2=Stuart |first2=Henry B. |journal=The Journal of Wildlife Management |date=1972 |volume=36 |issue=2 |pages=269–282 }}</ref> 3.2% of 7077 prey items from across the range for barred owls were [[Lagomorpha|rabbits or hares]].<ref name= Livezey5 /> Among cottontails, small juveniles are largely taken, but rabbits as large as an adult [[brush rabbit]]s can be taken.<ref name= Errington /><ref name= Graham /> The weights of snowshoe hares taken by barred owls in the Pacific Northwest were estimated at ranging from {{convert|50|g|lb|abbr=on}} leverets to juveniles and standard-sized adults about {{convert|1200|to|1400|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Wiens /><ref name= Graham /><ref name= Hamer />

[[Bat]]s are infrequently reported as prey in most of the range but an unusually close association was detected in [[Valdosta, Georgia]], where most of the prey, 65% of pellet contents and 37 total bats, were [[southeastern myotis]] (''Myotis austroriparius'').<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1656/058.016.0110 |title=Bats as Predominant Food Items of Nesting Barred Owls |date=2017 |last1=Bergstrom |first1=Bradley J. |last2=Smith |first2=Marvin T. |journal=Southeastern Naturalist |volume=16 |pages=N1–N4 |s2cid=89723777 }}</ref> Barred owls are also known predators of small mammalian [[carnivora]]ns, mainly [[mustelid]]s such as [[stoat]]s (''Mustela erminea'') and [[long-tailed weasel]]s (''Neogale frenata'').<ref>{{cite journal|title=Survival and predation of weasels (''Mustela erminea'', ''Mustela frenata'') in North America|date=2017 |doi=10.3955/046.091.0104 |last1=Linnell |first1=Mark A. |last2=Epps |first2=Clinton W. |last3=Forsman |first3=Eric D. |last4=Zielinski |first4=William J. |journal=Northwest Science |volume=91 |pages=15–26 |s2cid=89860715 }}</ref> Predation on small [[American mink]] (''N. vison'') have also been reported.<ref name= Errington/> As they are seemingly not affected by strong odor, [[eastern spotted skunk|eastern spotted]] and [[western spotted skunk]]s (''Spilogale gracilis'' & ''S. putorius'') of all ages can be taken.<ref name= Graham /><ref>{{cite journal|title=Eastern spotted skunk (''Spilogale putorius'') survival and cause-specific mortality in the Ouachita Mountains, Arkansas|date=2010 |doi=10.1674/0003-0031-164.1.52 |last1=Lesmeister |first1=Damon B. |last2=Millspaugh |first2=Joshua J. |last3=Gompper |first3=Matthew E. |last4=Mong |first4=Tony W. |journal=The American Midland Naturalist |volume=164 |pages=52–60 |s2cid=83621716 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1656/058.020.0sp1111 |title=Genetic Confirmation of Predation of an Adult Female Eastern Spotted Skunk by a Barred Owl |date=2021 |last1=Hassler |first1=Kendyl N. |last2=Kessinger |first2=Brin E. |last3=Harms |first3=Caroline E. |last4=Price |first4=Lucas E. |last5=Barton |first5=Ethan P. |last6=Oxenrider |first6=Kevin J. |last7=Rogers |first7=Rich E. |last8=Pearce |first8=Kelly J. |last9=Serfass |first9=Thomas L. |last10=Welsh |first10=Amy B. |journal=Southeastern Naturalist |volume=20 |s2cid=236999938 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1898/1051-1733-103.3.250 |title=Barred Owl Predation of Western Spotted Skunks |date=2022 |last1=Tosa |first1=Marie I. |last2=Lesmeister |first2=Damon B. |last3=Levi |first3=Taal |journal=Northwestern Naturalist |volume=103 |issue=3 |s2cid=253667556 }}</ref> Much larger mammals are sometimes recorded in the foods of barred owls, but there are few details known about the age, condition, or circumstances (i.e. they may have been consumed as [[carrion]] or, perhaps more likely, young or infirm specimens were taken).<ref name= Livezey5 /> Some such prey species recorded have included the [[Virginia opossum]], the [[North American porcupine]],<ref name= Marks /> the [[striped skunk]]<ref name= Voous /> and the [[Cat|domestic cat]]. Adults of all these species are known to count amongst the prey of great horned owls, which are better suited than barred owls to take particularly large prey, given its more robust morphology.<ref>{{cite book|last=Smith|first=Dwight G.|title=Great Horned Owl|year=2002|publisher=Stackpole Books|location=Mechanicsburg, PA |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=f1PVJVWbQKwC |isbn=978-0-8117-2689-4 |pages=33, 80–81 |edition=1st }}</ref>

=== Birds ===
[[File:Hermit thrush (27991992249).jpg|thumb|right|Barred owls will eat fledglings of the [[hermit thrush]]<ref name= Streby/>]]

Throughout the barred owl's range, other birds are taken as prey, although avian species make up a much smaller proportion of their diets than mammals. The maximum known representation of bird prey in a barred owl food study was 25.1% in [[Alberta]], meaning that they augment their diet less heavily with bird prey than their near equivalent in Europe, the [[tawny owl]].<ref name= BNA /><ref>Glue, D. E. (1972). ''Bird prey taken by British owls''. Bird Study, 19(2), 91–96.</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1002/ece3.3899 |title=What determines prey selection in owls? Roles of prey traits, prey class, environmental variables, and taxonomic specialization |date=2018 |last1=Comay |first1=Orr |last2=Dayan |first2=Tamar |journal=Ecology and Evolution |volume=8 |issue=6 |pages=3382–3392 |pmid=29607033 |pmc=5869362 |bibcode=2018EcoEv...8.3382C }}</ref> No specific variety of bird is subject to the most frequent predation by barred owls and birds are the most diverse class in these owl's prey spectrum, with more than 100 species of bird known to be hunted.<ref name= Bent /><ref name= Livezey5 /><ref name= Wiens /> Conspicuous nesting sites of [[barn swallow]] and [[purple martin]] on manmade structures and objects were revealed via video-monitoring to suffer heavy predation by barred owls. In the case of the barn swallows, all ages of swallow as well as possibly eggs were eaten. 65 of 95 monitoring barn swallow nests were consumed by owls over a 3-year period.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Evidence of Barred Owls (''Strix varia'') Depredating Nests of Barn Swallows (''Hirundo rustica'')|date=2017 |jstor=26456083|doi=10.1676/16-054.1 |last1=Mahony |first1=Nancy A. |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=129 |issue=3 |pages=641–644 |s2cid=89785198 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/292148462|author=Ray, J. D. |year=2014|title=Avian predation on Purple Martins nesting in artificial housing|journal= Bull. Texas Ornith. Soc|volume= 47|pages= 1–2}}</ref> The [[Bank (geography)|muddy bank]] nests of [[American cliff swallow|cliff swallows]] are also vulnerable to barred owls, while other swallow species are known to be opportunistically taken.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hamilton |first1=Gary D. |last2=Martin |first2=Robert F. |title=Investigator Perturbation and Reproduction of the Cliff Swallow |journal=The Auk |date=January 1985 |volume=102 |issue=1 |pages=167–170 |doi=10.2307/4086836|jstor=4086836 }}</ref>

In more enclosed wooded areas, radio-tagging and [[Camera trap|video-monitoring]] of various passerines nests as well as examinations of owl pellets has shed light on the relationship of barred owls with these potential prey resources. Not only was the barred owl found to be a surprisingly routine predator at woodland passerine nests, but that an unexpected bulk of the acts of predation in studies from Missouri and Illinois were carried out during the daytime. Many different forest bird species (most frequently [[Acadian flycatcher]]s and [[indigo bunting]]s in Missouri and Illinois) were hunted. These studies indicated that the barred owl may snatch passerines of any age, but recent fledglings are taken preferentially due to their more conspicuous behavior and limited ability to fly away. In Minnesota, about 62% of studied [[hermit thrush]] and [[ovenbird]] fledglings were taken per one study, with all thrush that nested in the {{convert|50|m|ft|abbr=on}} radius of the barred owl's nests failing to produce any young. A similarly high rate of local determent by barred owls has been found for other woodland thrushes like the [[veery]], [[wood thrush]] and [[varied thrush]], with the additional finding that pre-dawn singing by certain thrushes, when their escape abilities are dulled by the dim light, leaves them vulnerable to barred owl ambushes.<ref name= Takats /><ref name= Gill>{{cite journal |last1=Gill |first1=Ryan A. |last2=Cox |first2=W. Andrew |last3=Thompson |first3=Frank R. |title=Timing of Songbird Nest Predation as Revealed by Video Surveillance |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |date=March 2016 |volume=128 |issue=1 |pages=200–203 |doi=10.1676/1559-4491-128.1.200|s2cid=86980126 }}</ref><ref name= Streby>{{cite journal |last1=Streby |first1=Henry M. |last2=Refsnider |first2=Jeanine M. |last3=Peterson |first3=Sean M. |last4=Andersen |first4=David E. |title=Barred Owl Predation on Hermit Thrush and Ovenbird Fledglings |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |date=December 2008 |volume=42 |issue=4 |pages=296–298 |doi=10.3356/JRR-08-10.1|s2cid=83697804 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schmidt |first1=Kenneth A. |last2=Rush |first2=Scott A. |last3=Ostfeld |first3=Richard S. |title=Wood thrush nest success and post-fledging survival across a temporal pulse of small mammal abundance in an oak forest |journal=Journal of Animal Ecology |date=July 2008 |volume=77 |issue=4 |pages=830–837 |doi=10.1111/j.1365-2656.2008.01378.x|pmid=18355240 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2008JAnEc..77..830S }}</ref> Forest birds seem to recognize the barred owl as a threat, with mobbing behavior evoked easily by playing recordings of their calls in the daytime.
[[File:Ruffed Grouse (1).jpg|thumb|left|Ruffed grouse are known prey in extensive parts of the range]]

A wide diversity of bird prey may be occasionally hunted by barred owls in different circumstances. Smaller or mid-sized bird prey species known have including different species, though usually a relatively low species diversity and in low numbers, beyond swallows and thrushes of [[tyrant flycatcher]]s, [[vireo]]s, [[chickadee]]s, [[wren]]s, [[mimid]]s, [[Piranga|tanagers]], other [[Cardinalidae|cardinalids]] and [[finches]].<ref name= Bent /><ref name= Graham /> Somewhat higher diversity of species are known from the [[American sparrow|sparrow]] and [[New World warbler|warbler]] families.<ref name= Gill /><ref>Anich, N. M., T. J. Benson, J. D. Brown, C. Roa, J. C. Bednarz, R. E. Brown, and J. G. Dickson (2020). "Swainson's Warbler (''Limnothlypis swainsonii'')", version 1.0. In ''Birds of the World'' (P. G. Rodewald, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA.</ref><ref>Sherry, T. W., R. T. Holmes, P. Pyle, and M. A. Patten (2020). ''American Redstart (Setophaga ruticilla)'', version 1.0. In Birds of the World (P. G. Rodewald, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA.</ref> Birds down to the size of the [[calliope hummingbird]], North America's smallest hummingbird at {{convert|2.7|g|oz|abbr=on}}, may be taken by barred owls.<ref name= Wiens /> At the opposite end of passerine prey for barred owls, this species will sometimes take all ages of the [[American crow]], from very young nestlings to adults.<ref name= BNA /> Numerous non-passerine birds are also taken, though seldom in great numbers and of low known species diversity.<ref name= Livezey5 /> One exceptional family is the [[woodpecker]]s, which are probably so widely taken because of their generally overlapping habitat preferences with those of barred owls. Several species of woodpecker are preyed upon almost throughout the range, including at least a half dozen in Oregon alone, from the smallest North American species, the [[downy woodpecker]], to the largest, the [[pileated woodpecker]].<ref name= Graham /><ref name= Fisher>Fisher, A. K. (1893). ''The hawks and owls of the United States in their relation to agriculture'' (No. 3). US Department of Agriculture, Division of Ornithology and Mammalogy.</ref> Other small-to-medium-sized bird species known as prey for barred owls are: [[mountain quail]], [[grey partridge]], [[rock dove]], [[band-tailed pigeon]], [[mourning dove]], [[American purple gallinule|purple gallinule]], [[killdeer]], [[American woodcock]], [[least tern]], [[snowy egret]],<ref>Parsons, K. C. and T. L. Master (2020). "Snowy Egret (''Egretta thula'')", version 1.0. In ''Birds of the World'' (A. F. Poole and F. B. Gill, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA.</ref> [[cattle egret]]<ref>Telfair II, R. C. (2020). "Cattle Egret (''Bubulcus ibis''), version 1.0. In ''Birds of the World'' (S. M. Billerman, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA.</ref> and [[belted kingfisher]]. Although they take many chicks of gamebirds, adults of these species are vulnerable as well.<ref name= Bent /> In many areas, [[ruffed grouse]] are not infrequently taken, comprising up to nearly 6% of prey items in Alberta. In Oregon, the weights estimated for ruffed grouse taken by barred owls varied enormously, from small chicks estimated at {{convert|25|g|oz|abbr=on}}, to adults weighing about {{convert|576|g|lb|abbr=on}}. Broader study in the Pacific Northwest indicated that adult ruffed grouse were mainly taken.<ref name= Takats /><ref name= Wiens /><ref name= Graham /><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mangelinckx |first1=Joelle M. |last2=Brown |first2=Samantha R. |last3=Allen |first3=R. Bradford |last4=Sullivan |first4=Kelsey |last5=Blomberg |first5=Erik J. |title=Effects of forest characteristics on ruffed grouse nesting ecology in central Maine, USA |journal=Wildlife Biology |date=14 February 2020 |volume=2020 |issue=1 |doi=10.2981/wlb.00598|s2cid=211567501 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Barred owls are also known to take adult [[spruce grouse]] of about the same size as the ruffed grouse, as well as much larger species including the [[common pheasant]] and the [[sooty grouse]], the latter estimated to average {{convert|1050|g|lb|abbr=on}} when taken.<ref name= Marks /><ref name= Graham /><ref>Schroeder, M. A., E. J. Blomberg, D. A. Boag, P. Pyle, and M. A. Patten (2020). "Spruce Grouse (''Falcipennis canadensis'')", version 1.0. In ''Birds of the World'' (P. G. Rodewald, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA.</ref> Barred owls are also known to prey on the young of other, larger birds, such as the [[American white ibis]] and [[wild turkey]].<ref>Hingtgen, T. M., Mulholland, R., & Repenning, R. W. (1985). [https://www.usgs.gov/publications/habitat-suitability-index-models-white-ibis ''Habitat suitability index models: white ibis'']. Fish and Wildlife Service.</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1002/jwmg.21267 |title=Nest site selection and nest survival of eastern wild turkeys in a pyric landscape |date=2017 |last1=Yeldell |first1=Nathan A. |last2=Cohen |first2=Bradley S. |last3=Little |first3=Andrew R. |last4=Collier |first4=Bret A. |last5=Chamberlain |first5=Michael J. |journal=The Journal of Wildlife Management |volume=81 |issue=6 |pages=1073–1083 |bibcode=2017JWMan..81.1073Y |url=https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/natrespapers/731 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Other prey ===
Barred owl predation on reptiles is widely reported, but they seldom take large numbers in any given area.<ref name= Livezey5 /> Most reported instances of such captures are of various small [[lizard]]s, often of [[skink]]s in the genus ''[[Plestiodon]],'' most often from the [[Midwest]] to the western parts of the range.<ref name= Bent /><ref name= Blakemore /> Several reported instances of barred owls hunting [[snake]]s are also known, but they are perhaps even more seldom preyed upon than lizards.<ref name= Bent /><ref name= Graham /> More than a half dozen snake species are known to be captured, several of which are [[Colubridae|colubrids]], which are mostly harmless.<ref>Conant, R. (1975). ''A Field Guide to Reptiles and Amphibians of Eastern and Central North America, Second Edition''. Boston: Houghton Mifflin.</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Meshaka, W. E. Jr.|author2= Layne, J. N. |year=2015|url=https://www.herpconbio.org/Volume_10/Monographs/Meshaka_Layne_2015-small.pdf |title=The herpetology of southern Florida|journal=Herpetological Conservation and Biology|volume= 10|issue=5|pages=1–353}}</ref><ref>Platt, D.R. (1969). [https://webapps.fhsu.edu/ksherp/bibFiles/10.pdf ''Natural history of the hognose snakes Heterodon platyrhinos and Heterodon nasicus'']. Univ. Kansas Publ. Mus.</ref> The estimated body mass of [[Coluber constrictor|black racer]] taken in Oregon was only {{convert|77|g|oz|abbr=on}}, well under their mean mature size.<ref name="Graham" /> However, consumption or predation on dangerous [[pit viper]]s, such as [[timber rattlesnake]]s and [[Agkistrodon contortrix|copperheads]], by barred owls has been reported, although it is not known whether these are taken as adults.<ref name="Voous" /><ref name="Bent" /><ref>Ernst, C. H., & Ernst, E. M. (2011). ''Venomous Reptiles of the United States, Canada, and Northern Mexico: Crotalus (Vol. 2)''. JHU Press.</ref> Rarer still is barred owl predation on [[turtle]]s.<ref name= Livezey5 /> Predation by this species was reported upon a very young [[river cooter]], which had a carapace width of only {{convert|31.4|mm|in|abbr=on}}, as well as on juvenile [[gopher tortoise]] and apparently [[diamondback terrapin]].<ref name="Bent" /><ref>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/137555|author=Peterson, S.W. |year=1989|title=Barred owl eats hatchling Turtle|journal=Mississippi Kite|volume= 19 |issue=2|pages=4–5}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/1564605|title=Estimates of survival for juvenile gopher tortoises, ''Gopherus polyphemus''|jstor=1564605 |last1=Wilson |first1=Dawn S. |journal=Journal of Herpetology |date=1991 |volume=25 |issue=3 |pages=376–379 }}</ref> Much more characteristic than any reptilian prey are [[amphibian]]s, with various types of [[frog]]s, [[salamander]]s and similar species reported in this owl's foods.<ref name="Bent" /><ref name="Livezey5" /> Amphibians were considerably more popular in the diet in western part of range, comprising 10.5% of known studies against 4.4% in the east.<ref name="Livezey5" /> In total, well over 20 amphibian species have been identified in the foods of barred owls and amphibians collectively can make up to 24.5% of the local diet (as was reported in Alberta).<ref name="BNA" /><ref name="Takats" /><ref name="Livezey5" /> While salamanders and newts are probably often visually discerned while scanning the forest floor, many frogs are probably hunted down by sound during their crepuscular choruses.<ref name="Johnsgard" />

Amphibians are taken almost entirely during the breeding season, as they become unavailable to barred owls during the winter months.<ref name="Wiens" /><ref name="Graham" /> Most reported amphibian prey in Oregon were unidentified "medium-sized salamanders". A diversity of frog sizes may be taken, varying potentially in size from [[spring peeper]]s, which average around {{convert|4|g|oz|abbr=on}}, to [[American bullfrog]]s, which average around in mature bullfrogs {{convert|430|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://animals.nationalgeographic.com/animals/amphibians/spring-peeper.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070614110143/http://animals.nationalgeographic.com/animals/amphibians/spring-peeper.html|archive-date=June 14, 2007|title=Spring Peeper Profile|publisher=[[National Geographic Society]]|access-date=2020-04-21}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=Frost, D.R. |author2=Grant, T. |author3=Faivovich, J. |author4=Bain, R.H. |author5=Haas, A. |author6=Haddad, C.F.B. |author7=De Sá, R.O. |author8=Channing, A. |author9=Wilkinson, M. |author10=Donnellan, S.C. |author11=Raxworthy, C.J. |author12=Campbell, J.A. |author13=Blotto, B.L. |author14=Moler, P. |author15=Drewes, R.C. |author16=Nussbaum, R.A. |author17=Lynch, J.D. |author18=Green, D.M. |author19=Ward, C. |author20=Wheeler, W.C. |title=The Amphibian Tree of Life|journal=Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History|volume=297|pages=1–291|doi=10.1206/0003-0090(2006)297[0001:TATOL]2.0.CO;2|hdl=2246/5781|year=2006|s2cid=86140137 |doi-access=free }}</ref> A notable act of successful predation was carried out by a barred owl on a [[rough-skinned newt]], which contains [[toxin]]s that are often deadly to predators. Apparently, the owl was able to survive after consuming the newt.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Rough-skinned newt (''Taricha granulosa'') predation by Barred Owls (''Strix varia'')|date=2018 |doi=10.1676/17-052.1 |last1=Medina |first1=Shelby L. |last2=Higley |first2=Mark |last3=Dumbacher |first3=John P. |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=130 |issue=3 |pages=780–783 |s2cid=91731949 }}</ref> The rarest variety of vertebrate prey for barred owls is fish.<ref name= Livezey5 /> However, there are several accrued accounts of fishing by these owls, including older accounts of barred owls coming to [[Ice fishing|ice holes]] made by human [[fishermen]] and more recent accounts of possibly routine fishing by the owls on the [[St. Johns River]] in Florida, in the latter case utilizing a fishing [[dock (maritime)|dock]] as a hunting perch.<ref name= Bent /><ref>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jfo/v054n01/p0088-p0089.pdf|author1=Smith, D. G.|author2=Devine, A.|author3= Devine, D. |year=1983|title=Observations of fishing by a Barred Owl|journal=Journal of Field Ornithology|pages= 88–89}}</ref> At least five species of fish have been identified in the foods of barred owls, including fairly large fish like [[brown bullhead]]s and [[largemouth bass]].<ref name= Voous /><ref name= Bent /><ref name= Graham />

[[File:Wood Frog - Lithobates sylvaticus, Lake Accotink Park, Springfield, Virginia (39440713811).jpg|thumb|left|Frogs such as [[wood frog]]s are a regular prey source for barred owls]]

The balance of assorted [[invertebrate]]s in the diet of barred owls can be quite high. Although many of those found in pellets are unidentified to species, nearly 40 species of invertebrates have been found in their foods.<ref name= Bent /><ref name= Livezey5 /><ref name= Graham /> Broad studies indicate [[arthropod]]s (including [[millipede]]s and [[spider]]s but predominantly [[insect]]s) in general comprise up to about 16% of the barred owl's foods.<ref name= Snyder /><ref name= Wiens /> When hunting [[insect]]s, barred owls most often prefer ground-based [[beetle]]s. In Oregon, 11.7% of the diet was assorted beetles (14 species were identified), being somewhat more numerous among 3686 prey items than other non-rodent prey orders.<ref name= Graham /> However, some insects prey such as [[luna moth]], [[Papilio glaucus|eastern tiger swallowtail]] and [[green darner]] are presumably [[Hawking (birds)|hawked]] on the wing around dawn and dusk.<ref name="Voous" /><ref name="Livezey5" /> It is known that barred owls will sometimes come hunt near manmade light sources and [[campfire]]s, flying out from the perch to quickly capture flying insects that were attracted to them.<ref name="Bent" /><ref name="Fisher" /> Of 123 prey items found in southern Manitoba, flying [[Sphingidae|Sphinx moths]] and flightless [[Phyllophaga|scarab beetles]] each comprised 7% (most of the remaining balance being unidentified mammals and birds). [[Crayfish]] of at least four identified species are another widely taken type of invertebrate, presumably snatched up by barred owls from shallow waterways.<ref name="Livezey5" /><ref name="Graham" /><ref name="Blakemore" /><ref>{{cite journal|jstor=24321812|author=VanArman, P. G. |year=2011|title=Role of native crayfish, '''Procambarus alleni'' (Faxon) and ''Procambarus fallax'' (Hagen), in everglades food webs: a literature review and conceptual model|journal= Florida Scientist|pages= 100–125|volume= 74|issue= 2}}</ref> Crayfish seem to be exclusively taken in the east and Midwest, areas where they comprised 3.4% of all known food studies, and none at all were recorded in the western part of the range.<ref name="Livezey5" /> In [[Missouri]], annually up to 31.1% (though, in some years, 0%) of the diet comprised crayfish. Barred owls occasionally feed on [[snail]]s and [[slug]]s, the latter especially in the Pacific Northwest.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Worm /> Another snail, the [[Monadenia fidelis|Pacific sideband]], was surprisingly often present in the foods in Oregon as at least 135 were taken.<ref name= Graham /> Beyond slugs, other "soft" invertebrates are sometimes hunted, especially [[earthworm]]s. Earthworms were most prominent in the foods of barred owls in [[Nova Scotia]], where 27.6% of 186 video-monitored prey deliveries in Nova Scotia were worms, the most regularly delivered of all prey types there.<ref name= Elderkin /><ref name= Worm />

=== Interspecies predatory relationships ===
The barred owl's range overlaps with multiple other predators of similar prey species. Due to the time period barred owls are active, the most interaction occurs with other owl species. Of the owls in North America, about three-quarters are reliant on similar small mammal prey, usually rodents, with a mixture of other prey genera as supplements.<ref name= Weidensaul /> Other than its ecology where it today co-exists with spotted owls, however, there is some level of [[niche differentiation]] from a majority of sympatric owls.<ref name= Voous /> In the eastern forest biome, the barred owl is the only large owl species to dwell mainly in continuous forest areas.<ref name= Voous /><ref name= BNA /><ref name= Marti /> While many of the owls sympatric with barred owls over majority of their eastern and Midwestern range share a preference for hole-nesting, smaller hole-nesting owls usually prefer different habitats, such as the open country-dwelling [[American barn owl]] and the [[screech owls]], which usually in North America dwell at the interface of forest and open habitats.<ref name= Voous /><ref name= Johnsgard /> Both North American species in the ''[[Aegolius]]'' owl genus are forest-dwelling cavity nesters but are much smaller and are at entirely different [[trophic level]]s.<ref name= Voous /><ref name= Johnsgard /><ref name= Grossman />

A singular diurnal raptor species that mirrors the barred owl at nearly all ecological levels is the [[red-shouldered hawk]]. Both species have similar distributions, habitat preferences and somewhat similar dietary habits and trophic level. They may considered as nearly nocturnal and diurnal [[Ecological niche|ecological equivalents]].<ref name= BNA /><ref>Portnoy, J. W., & Dodge, W. E. (1979). ''Red-shouldered Hawk nesting ecology and behavior''. The Wilson Bulletin, 104–117.</ref> In multiple parts of the range, including southwestern Ohio, North Carolina and northern Michigan, the paralleling habitat usage and nesting behavior of the barred owl and red-shouldered hawk has been noted. One of the few respects in which their habitat usage differs is that the barred owl is more adaptive to nesting in suburban areas if they have old growth trees that provide plentiful [[tree hollow]]s. In contrast, red-shouldered hawks tend to avoid suburban areas whilst nesting, but may acclimate to these areas during the winter. Barred owls seldom alter their range throughout the year and remain more or less constrained to the stands they used during nesting.<ref name= Dykstra /><ref>Mason, J.S. (2004). [https://www.ospreytrax.com/Osprey_Migration/abstract.htm ''The reproductive success, survival, and natal dispersal of Barred Owls (Strix varia) in rural versus urban habitats in and around Charlotte, North Carolina'']. M.S. thesis, University of NC.</ref><ref name= Postupalsky>Postupalsky, S., Paap, J. M. & Scheller, L. (1997). "Nest sites and reproductive success of Barred Owls (''Strix varia'') in Michigan". Pages 325–337 in ''Biology and Conservation of Owls in the Northern Hemisphere: Second International Symposium''. J. R. Duncan, D. H. Johnson, and T. H. Nicholls (eds). USDA Forest Service General Technical Report NC-190.</ref> The red-shouldered hawk averages somewhat smaller and has a more limited diet than barred owls, but probably goes after dangerous prey such as snakes more regularly.<ref name= Voous /><ref name= Bent /><ref>{{cite journal|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/56868268#page/82/mode/1up|author=Roble, S. M. |year=2013|title=Ophiophagy in Red-shouldered Hawks (''Buteo lineatus''), with the First Record of Eastern Wormsnakes (''Carphophis amoenus'') as Prey|journal= Banisteria |volume=41|pages=80–84}}</ref> Multiple occasions are recorded where the barred owl and red-shouldered hawk have nested in the same area, often within the same grove of trees, remarkably with little to no conflict.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Johnsgard /> At least one nest was found including eggs from both species.<ref name= Bent /> When nesting near other hawks like [[red-tailed hawk]]s and [[Cooper's hawk]]s, the relationship tends to be much more contentious between hawk and owl, despite barred owls sometimes sharing space or using the old nests of these hawks.<ref name= Voous /> A wing-clapping display by a pair of barred owls was recorded during antagonistic encounters in Manitoba with a pair of [[broad-winged hawk]]s. Wing-claps were previously not recorded in this species, and when recorded for other owl species were often for the purposes of courtship displays.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Occurrences of wing clapping behavior in Barred Owls (''Strix varia'')|date=2012 |doi=10.3356/JRR-12-18.1 |last1=Whiklo |first1=Todd M. |last2=Duncan |first2=James R. |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |volume=46 |issue=4 |pages=413–416 |s2cid=86567181 }}</ref>

[[File:Great Horned Owl on Seedskadee National Wildlife Refuge (22312850906).jpg|thumb|left|[[Great horned owl]]s are the most severe competitor and predator of barred owls]]

One predator that is a major source of conflict for the barred owl is the [[great horned owl]].<ref name= BNA /> In every part of their range, barred owls are compelled to share space with the larger owls.<ref name= Voous /> There is habitat partitioning between the barred owl and great horned owl that allows them to often co-exist. Great horned owls prefer various more open habitats mixed with trees, often in rather upland areas, which differs from the habitats preferred by the barred owl.<ref name= Voous /><ref name= Johnsgard /> If a great horned owl moves into an area, barred owls appear to avoid said areas, based on radio telemetry data.<ref name= Laidig /> In much of the east, habitat alteration and fragmentation tends to favor the great horned owl at the expense of the barred owl. Where more continuous forest is available, however, the great horned and barred owl can occur surprisingly close to one another. In one case, a barred owl was observed to roost only {{convert|400|m|ft|abbr=on}} from a great horned owl.<ref name= Laidig /><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/4079048|jstor=4079048 |last1=Baumgartner |first1=Frederick M. |title=Territory and Population in the Great Horned Owl |journal=The Auk |date=1939 |volume=56 |issue=3 |pages=274–282 |doi-access=free }}</ref> In general the [[reforestation]] at the northern sections of the [[Great Plains]] have, on the contrary, benefited barred owls (and may have been a part of allowing their westward expansion) and been perhaps slightly detrimental to the great horned owl.<ref name= Holt /> However, even where the habitat becomes less ideal, the great horned owl is unlikely to vacate an area, unlike the barred owl which can be entirely displaced if woods become too small and fragmented.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Duncan /> Great horned owls and barred owls have similar diets, as both are wide-ranging, large and opportunistic owls.<ref name= Weidensaul /><ref name= Johnsgard /> However, the great horned owl is larger in bulk, sometimes averaging nearly twice as heavy, with much heavier, larger feet and talons.<ref name= Voous /><ref name= Snyder /> The great horned owl has a more powerful grip strength<ref name= Ward /> and access to a wider variety of prey than barred owls, which take fewer prey species because they occur in more limited habitats and have a smaller overall distribution.<ref name= Voous /><ref name= Bent /><ref name= Marti /><ref>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v076n01/p0045-p0061.pdf|author=Marti, C. D. |year=1974|title=Feeding ecology of four sympatric owls|journal= The Condor|volume= 76|issue=1|pages=45–61|doi=10.2307/1365983 |jstor=1365983 }}</ref><ref name= Earhart/> A study utilizing [[Stable isotope ratio|stable isotopes]] in Alberta appeared to confirm that the great horned owl preys on nearly twice as many prey species as do the barred owls of the area.<ref>Duxbury, J. M., & Holroyd, G. L. (1997). "You are what you eat: stable isotope ecology of owl diets in Alberta, Canada". In: Duncan, James R.; Johnson, David H.; Nicholls, Thomas H., eds. ''Biology and conservation of owls of the Northern Hemisphere: 2nd International symposium''. Gen. Tech. Rep. NC-190. St. Paul, MN: US Dept. of Agriculture, Forest Service, North Central Forest Experiment Station. 148–154. (Vol. 190).</ref>

The great horned owl is indeed likely to be the greatest natural enemy of the barred owl.<ref name= Voous /><ref name= Bent /> There are several accounts of the horned owl species preying on nestlings, fledglings and adults of the barred.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Bent /><ref name= Fuller /><ref>{{cite journal|url=http://www.wildlifebc.org/pdfs/2_2_VanDamme.pdf|author = Van Damme, L. M. |year=2005|title=Diet of the great horned owl in the Creston Valley, British Columbia, 1998–2005|journal= Wildlife Afield|volume= 2 |issue=2|pages=73–78}}</ref> Other than horned owls, predation of the barred owl seems to be fairly rare outside of the nestling age, both due to the owl's relatively large size and their nesting habits, most often in secluded tree hollows.<ref name= BNA /> One more virulent nest predator is likely to be [[raccoon]]s (''Procyon lotor''), which can nearly decimate both tree hollow and stick nest locations of almost any kind of bird, especially in [[Peri-urbanisation|peri-urban]] areas where they largely exist without controlling predators.<ref name= Elderkin /><ref>Haskell, D. G., Knupp, A. M., & Schneider, M. C. (2001). "Nest predator abundance and urbanization", pp. 243–258 in ''Avian ecology and conservation in an urbanizing world''. Springer, Boston, MA. {{doi|10.1007/978-1-4615-1531-9_11}} {{ISBN|978-1-4615-1531-9}}</ref> Both the [[American marten]] and the [[Fisher (animal)|fisher]] are known to be nest predators of barred owls, in turn appearing to cause the owls to switch nest sites.<ref>Mazur, K. M., James, P. C., & Frith, S. D. (1997). [https://research.fs.usda.gov/treesearch/15497 "Barred Owl (''Strix varia'') nest site characteristics in the boreal forest of Saskatchewan, Canada"]. US DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE FOREST SERVICE GENERAL TECHNICAL REPORT NC, 267–271.</ref> Other birds of prey may be an occasional threat to barred owls. [[American goshawk]]s have reportedly killed both young and adult barred owls.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Bent /> One modern account mentions predation by a goshawk on a nestling barred owl (i.e. weight about {{convert|392|g|oz|abbr=on}}).<ref>{{cite journal|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228635407|author1=Smithers, B. L.|author2= Boal, C. W.|author3=Andersen, D. A. |year=2005|title=Northern Goshawk diet in Minnesota: an analysis using video recording systems|journal=Journal of Raptor Research|volume= 39|issue=3|page=264}}</ref> A well-known instance of a goshawk attack on an adult barred owl concluded with the owl and goshawk killing one another.<ref name= Voous /><ref>{{cite journal|author=Eifrig, G. |year=1907|title=American Goshawk (''Accipiter atricapillus'') versus Man and Barred Owl|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/8564|journal=The Auk|pages= 437–438}}</ref> There are some very rare, singular cases of predation on barred owls (age unknown) by [[red-tailed hawk]]s, which nonetheless generally appears to be a less menacing co-inhabitant than the goshawk.<ref name= Bent /><ref name= Shupe>{{cite journal|url=https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2218&context=nebbirdrev|author=Shupe, S. |year=1985|title=Observations of the Barred Owl in southeastern Nebraska|journal=Nebraska Bird Review|pages= 37–38}}</ref> More surprisingly, a [[Cooper's hawk]], a smaller species of raptor, was observed to apparently prey upon a full-grown barred owl in British Columbia.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Stirling, D. |year=2011|title=Cooper's Hawk Feeding on Barred Owl and Norway Rat in Victoria, British Columbia|journal= Wildlife Afield |volume=8|issue=1|pages=120–122|url=http://www.wildlifebc.org/pdfs/aug%202012/Stirling8.1Red.pdf}}</ref> An [[American alligator]] was reported to have preyed on a barred owl in at least one instance.<ref>Neill, W. T. (1971). ''The Last of the Ruling Reptiles''. Columbia University Press, New York, NY, USA.</ref>

On the other hand, the barred owl is a significant predator of smaller raptor species as well. Most smaller owls that co-exist with barred owls are at occasional risk of predation.<ref name= Voous /> A particular cause of concern in intraguild predations{{Definition needed|date=July 2020}} by the barred has been their encroachment into the areas inhabited by [[western screech owl]] (''Megascops kennicotti''). Several instances of predation have been noted on the screech owls and the recent, unprecedented presence of the barred owls appears to have a correlation with the decline of the western screech owl in British Columbia and in [[Bainbridge Island, Washington]].<ref name= Graham /><ref>{{cite journal|url=https://bcbirds.files.wordpress.com/2016/08/bcbirds_v14_p2_declining-numbers-of-western-screech-owl-in-the-lower-mainland-of-british-columbia.pdf|author=Elliott, K. |year=2006|title=Declining numbers of Western Screech-Owl in the lower mainland of British Columbia|journal= British Columbia Birds|volume= 14|pages= 2–11}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1898/nwn11-21.1 |title=Recent Trends in Western Screech-Owl and Barred Owl Abundances on Bainbridge Island, Washington |date=2012 |last1=Acker |first1=Jamie |journal=Northwestern Naturalist |volume=93 |issue=2 |pages=133–137 |s2cid=85889087 }}</ref> In one instance, a biologist who called in an [[eastern screech owl]] (''Megascops asio'') in Ohio observed it to be immediately captured and consumed by a barred owl. The barred owl is a serious predator of eastern screech owls, but is less deadly to them in general than the great horned owl.<ref>Ritchison, G., F. R. Gehlbach, P. Pyle, and M. A. Patten (2020). "Eastern Screech-Owl (''Megascops asio'')", version 1.0. In ''Birds of the World''. P. G. Rodewald (Ed.). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA.</ref> Various additional owl species known to be preyed upon by barred owls including the [[flammulated owl]] (''Psiloscops flammeolus''),<ref>Linkhart, B. D. and D. A. McCallum (2020). "Flammulated Owl (''Psiloscops flammeolus'')", version 1.0. In Birds of the World (A. F. Poole, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA.</ref> [[northern pygmy owl]] (''Glaucidium gnoma''),<ref name="Wiens" /> [[northern saw-whet owl]] (''Aegolius acadius'')<ref name= Graham /> and [[long-eared owl]] (''Asio otus'').<ref name="Bent" /> The interspecific owl trophic chain was perhaps most conspicuous in a case where a barred owl that was shot in New England was found to have a long-eared owl inside its stomach which in turn had an eastern screech-owl in its own stomach.<ref name="Voous" /><ref name="Bent" /> More unexpectedly, barred owls may even prey on other ''Strix'' species. As many as four records of circumstantial but likely predation by barred owls on [[spotted owl]]s have been reported.<ref name="Wiens" /> A likely event of predation by a barred owl on an adult [[great grey owl]] was observed. The authors hypothesized that the victim may have been a smaller male great grey owl (which can be about the same body mass as a large female barred) but this is the only known instance to date of a great gray owl being killed by another species of ''Strix'' owl.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.3356/0892-1016(2006)40[175:PPOGGO]2.0.CO;2|issn=0892-1016 |date=2006 |volume=40 |page=175 |title=Possible Predation of Great Gray Owl by a Barred Owl |last1=Graves |first1=Gary R. |last2=Niemi |first2=Gerald J. |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |issue=2 |s2cid=86070731 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Barred owls have been known to take adults (or full-grown juveniles) of diurnal raptors as well, including [[snail kite]]s, [[Cooper's hawk]]s, [[sharp-shinned hawk]]s and possibly [[swallow-tailed kite]]s.<ref name= Fisher /><ref>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jfo/v058n02/p0171-p0189.pdf|author=Sykes, P.W. Jr. |year=1987|title=Some aspects of the breeding biology of the Snail Kite in Florida|journal=J. Field Ornithol.|volume= 58|pages= 171–189|jstor=4513216}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author=Hertzel, A.X. |year=2003|title=Barred Owl takes Cooper's Hawk?|journal=Loon |volume=75|page=58}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.3356/JRR-07-08.1 |title=Predators of the Swallow-Tailed Kite in Southern Louisiana and Mississippi |date=2008 |last1=Coulson |first1=Jennifer O. |last2=Coulson |first2=Thomas D. |last3=Defrancesch |first3=Sherry A. |last4=Sherry |first4=Thomas W. |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |volume=42 |pages=1–12 |s2cid=84286796 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

== Reproduction ==
[[File:Two-owls-perched-branch - West Virginia - ForestWander.jpg|thumb|right|A probable mated pair of barred owl adults in daytime]]

The barred owl's preferred nest site is usually the [[Tree hollow|hollow trunk]] of a large tree or the broken-off [[Snag (ecology)|snag]] from a large tree branch. Hollows or snags may be made by any variety of sources, often due to a [[Plant pathology|disease]] or storm, with hollows and snags large enough to accommodate these birds usually only occurring in an old-growth tree.<ref name= Hume /><ref name= Bent /><ref name= Peck /><ref name= Devereux /> Typically, nest sites are in rather deep and dark wooded areas, often with a well-developed understory but somewhat sparse lower branches, and may be fairly close to water.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Applegate /> Average nest heights are between {{convert|6.8|and|13.4|m|ft|abbr=on}} above the ground.<ref name= BNA /> In 25 studies from throughout the range, mean nest tree height was {{convert|18.2|m|ft|abbr=on}} (lowest mean from Florida where it was {{convert|5.9|m|ft|abbr=on}}; the highest from Oregon where it was {{convert|28|m|ft|abbr=on}}), mean nest tree diameter was {{convert|65.7|cm|in|abbr=on}} (thinnest mean in Saskatchewan at {{convert|47.4|cm|in|abbr=on}}; thickest in Washington at {{convert|106|cm|in|abbr=on}}), while the mean height of the tree cavity used from these studies was {{convert|9.8|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Livezey5 />

A big [[beech]] with a rotting core, encompassing a large cavity reached through a deep crevice, is an ideal nest site.<ref name= Hume /> The most widely reported nesting trees in breeding cards were [[elm]]s (21%) and beeches (15%), followed by [[oak]]s, [[Hickory|hickories]], [[Betula alleghaniensis|yellow birches]], [[Platanus occidentalis|sycamores]], [[aspen]]s, [[maple]]s and [[Populus|poplars]].<ref name= Apfelbaum>{{cite journal|url=https://biostor.org/reference/216633|author1=Apfelbaum, S. I. |author2= Seelbach, P. |year=1983|title=Nest Tree, Habitat Selection and Productivity of Seven North American Raptor Species Based on the Cornell University Nest Record Card Program|journal= Raptor Research|volume= 17|issue=4|pages=97–113}}</ref> In Washington, the most often used nest trees were [[Populus balsamifera|balsam poplar]], [[Pseudotsuga menziesii|Douglas fir]] and [[Abies grandis|grand fir]].<ref name="Buchanan" /> Balsam poplar comprised 62% of known nest sites in Manitoba as well.<ref name="Whiklo2" /> It was found in Maryland that snags used were significantly higher than the average height of miscellaneous snags available in environment. The mean size of tree holes the owls used in Maryland was {{convert|33|cm|in|abbr=on}} in inside diameter and {{convert|54|cm|in|abbr=on}} in depth.<ref name="Devereux" /> These were slightly larger than the average from Michigan, which hollow used averaged {{convert|25|cm|in|abbr=on}} in mean diameter and {{convert|35|cm|in|abbr=on}} in mean depth.<ref name="Postupalsky" /> Record depth tree cavities used by barred owls was around {{convert|2.4|m|ft|abbr=on}} deep from the opening on the tree.<ref name="Bent" /> One study of cavity nesting birds in Ontario found that the barred owl preferred to nest in the most massive trees of any cavity-nester in the study area.<ref>James, R. D. (1984). [https://docs.ontario.ca/documents/2793/guide-cavity.pdf ''Habitat management guidelines for cavity-nesting birds in Ontario'']. Ontario ministry of natural resources.</ref> In continuous bottomland forest, nests are often about every {{convert|226|ha|acre|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{cite journal|jstor=4159308|author=Bell, R. E. |year=1964|title=A sound-triangulation method for counting Barred Owls|journal=The Wilson Bulletin|volume=76|issue=3|pages= 292–294}}</ref> Territory lines often remain the same even after the original owls are replaced entirely by a new pair.<ref name= Nicholls /> It was thought in Nova Scotia that some pairs may prospect a potential nest site as much as year before they use it.<ref name= Elderkin /> Despite usually using sickly or dying trees, some nests have found in partially hollow but still living [[oak]]s.<ref name= Konig />

[[File:Barred-owl-chick-17b.jpg|thumb|left|Barred owl nestlings peer out of the typical nesting site, a spacious tree hollow]]

Usually in areas with few or no natural tree hollows, often within younger [[secondary forest]]s or [[Overexploitation|overharvested]] areas, this species will uses other [[Nest|birds' nests]] and occasionally also the [[drey]]s of squirrels.<ref name= Konig /><ref name= Hume /><ref name= Apfelbaum /> Evidence shows that the preference of barred owls for hollows and snags over bird nests is due to their earlier nest type having a more secure [[microclimate]] with better shelter (additionally, owl nests in hollows generally tend to be somewhat less vulnerable to predation than those of owls using old bird nests).<ref name= Bent /><ref>{{cite journal|author1=McComb, W. C.|author2= Noble, R. E. |year=1981|title=Microclimates of nest boxes and natural cavities in bottomland hardwoods|journal= The Journal of Wildlife Management|volume= 45|issue=1|pages=284–289|doi= 10.2307/3807906 |jstor= 3807906 }}</ref> Nest built by other birds that are most widely used are probably [[red-shouldered hawk]] and [[Cooper's hawk]]s, while those of [[red-tailed hawk]]s, usually being in more open areas, are used secondarily outside of a local basis.<ref name= Voous /><ref name= Shupe /><ref>{{cite book  | last = Sattler | first = Helen Roney  | author-link = Helen Roney Sattler  | title = The Book of North American Owls  | publisher = Clarion Books  | year = 1995  | page = 41  | url = https://archive.org/details/bookofnorthameri0000satt/page/41| url-access = registration | isbn = 0-395-60524-5}}</ref> In the south, nests have been found between fronds of [[Sabal|palmetto]] palm leaves, in holes of broken palm stems and [[Snag (ecology)|rotten snags]] of the palms.<ref name= Konig /> One unconventional nest was on the ground at the base of a lookout tower in [[Everglades National Park]].<ref name= Robertson>{{cite journal|author=Robertson, W. B. |year=1959|title=Barred Owl nesting on the ground|journal= The Auk|volume= 76|issue=2|pages=227–230|doi=10.2307/4081780 |jstor=4081780 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Another was on the roof of a shed in Saskatchewan.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Houston, C. S. |year=1999|title=Barred Owl nest in attic of shed|journal= Wilson Bulletin |volume=111 |issue=2|pages=272–273}}</ref> Yet another was in an [[Bank (geography)|earthen bank]] in Texas.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Shackelford, C. E. |year=1996|title=Barred Owl nest in a natural hole in an earthen bank in eastern Texas|journal= J. Raptor Res. |volume=30 |issue=1|page=41}}</ref> Locally, barred owls can take to [[nest box]]es but, in general, barred owls take to these less readily than their cousin ''Strix'' owls in Europe.<ref name= Konig /><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Johnson, D. H.|author2= Follen, D. G. |year=1984|title=Barred Owls and nest boxes|journal= Raptor Res|volume= 18|issue=1|pages=34–35|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jrr/v018n01/p00034-p00035.pdf}}</ref> Suitable nesting hollows may be used quite often in subsequent years, with records of a single hollow seeing up to 25 years of barred owl use (presumably not by the same owls however).<ref name= Konig /> Other long-used nests were one reused in New England for 10 years by the same pair until it rotted out and while a nest box in Nova Scotia was reused 10 times over 16 years.<ref name= Bent /><ref name= Elderkin /> Over 6 years in Minnesota, 14 nest boxes were reused; 7 were used once, 6 were twice and one was used three times.<ref name= Johnson2>Johnson, D. H. (1987). "Barred Owls and Nest Boxes-Results of a Five-Year Study In Minnesota". In ''Biology and Conservation of Northern Forest Owls: Symposium Proceedings'': February 3–7, 1987, Winnipeg, Manitoba (Vol. 142, No. 3, p. 128). Fort Collins, Colo.: Rocky Mountain Forest and Range Experiment Station.</ref> Like all owls, barred owls never construct their owl nest but they may press or dig slightly if soil is present or remove the top leaves from a squirrel [[drey]].<ref name= Bent /><ref name= Elderkin />

In normal circumstances, barred owls tend to pair bond for life.<ref name= BNA /> Courting pairs are usually newly mature birds but also possibly widowed owls or those re-establishing existing pair bonds after the winter.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Bent /> During courtship, barred owl males especially may engage in nodding, bowing with half-spread wings and may wobble and twist their head from side to side.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Bent /> Courting pairs will often engage in duets.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Barred Owl {{!}} Audubon Field Guide |url=https://www.audubon.org/field-guide/bird/barred-owl |access-date=2023-12-14 |website=www.audubon.org |language=en}}</ref> [[Copulation (zoology)|Copulation]] between pairs usually occurs in late winter, February to March, and occurs several times, probably to ensure implantation.<ref>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/154-164%20notes%20OB%20Vol%2022%233%20Dec%202004.pdf|author=Robinson, C. |year=2004|title=Barred Owl Mating Behaviour|journal= Ontario Birds|volume=22 |issue=3|pages=154–156}}</ref> The female enters a pre-breeding lethargy stage wherein she is fed by the male.<ref name= Elderkin /> In barred owls, egg laying occurs at two to three day intervals.<ref name= Hume /> Egg laying typically begins in March and runs throughout April. In more tropical locales like Florida, egg laying may occur as early as late December, though 22 were laid in between early January and early March. At similar latitudes in Texas, 22 first eggs were laid between as early as mid-February to as late as early June (although the latter may have been a replacement clutch).<ref name= BNA /> 23 clutches in Iowa and Illinois were initiated in between late February and late April, with roughly corresponding initial egg dates in New Jersey and probably western Maryland as well.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Bosakowski /><ref name= Devereux /> 63 initial eggs in New England were laid between mid-March and mid-May.<ref name= Bent /> Further north, 38 clutches in Nova Scotia were initiated in between late March and late May, with initial clutches dates about a week later in Ontario.<ref name= Peck /><ref name= Elderkin /> Normally the female lays 2 to 3 eggs, although as many as 5 is possible, directly on the base of the nest site.<ref name= Konig /> The mean clutch size in three broad samples from several parts of the range was between 2.22 and 2.46.<ref name= Bent /><ref name= Apfelbaum /><ref>{{cite journal|author=Murray, G. A. |year=1976|title=Geographic variation in the clutch size of seven owl species|journal= Auk |volume=93|issue=3 |pages= 602–613|jstor=4084960}}</ref> In Minnesota, the mean clutch size was 2.68.<ref name= Johnson2 /> The eggs are pure white, slightly rough and not glossy, and oval in shape.<ref name= BNA /> In 157 eggs, the length of the egg could measure from {{convert|42.5|to|55.5|mm|in|abbr=on}} while the diameter could range from {{convert|37.5|to|45.3|mm|in|abbr=on}}, with a mean of {{convert|50.6|×|43.3|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Bent /> 25 eggs in Nova Scotia averaged slightly smaller, at {{convert|49|x|41|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Elderkin /> The mean weight of the egg is around {{convert|45.5|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Elderkin /> The female alone incubates, doing so for about 28 days, while the male gathers food for her.<ref name= Konig />

The female tends to closely brood the young for three weeks. Then after, she begins to hunt for the young. Both parents normally continue hunt for the young until they are about 6 weeks old and can fly well.<ref name= Hume /> During the first three weeks, the male does all prey deliveries, either bringing prey directly into the nest or leaves for the female at the nest entrance or on a nearby branch. The mean number of prey deliveries when the young are 6–10 days old is 2.4 per night, 1.4 when they are 11–15 days old, 3.6 when they are 16–20 days old and 2.2 when they are 21–25 days old. He will continue hunting until the young disperse.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Elderkin /><ref name= Robertson /> The female's prey deliveries are much more frequent when she resumes hunting, ranging from a mean of 4.8 to 8 deliveries nightly.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Elderkin /> Male prey deliveries are often sporadically at any point through the night while female deliveries tend to be clustered immediately after sunset or just before sunrise.<ref name= Elderkin /> Barred owls have a variable reaction to human disturbance. Some parent owls remain sitting, while some leave the nest upon a person's approach and some attack people climbing to the nest, as well as those approaching young on or near the ground.<ref name= Bent /><ref name= Dunstan /><ref name= Henderson>{{cite journal|author=Henderson, G. |year=1933|title=Boldness of Barred Owls when danger threatens young|journal= Wilson Bulletin |volume=45|page=143|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v045n03/p0143-p0143.pdf}}</ref> There are several accounts of barred owls engaging in fierce nest defense against humans, sometimes reportedly knocking people out of trees.<ref name= Voous /><ref name= Bent /><ref name= Henderson /> However, their aggression is variable and, based on studies of similar owls like tawny and Ural owls from Europe, attacks tend to only occur when intrusions and disturbance by humans are frequent. A distraction display was recorded by a female barred owl when humans approached. In it, she spread and quivered her wings and engaged in chittering and squealing, somewhat similar to the sounds made by begging young.<ref>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/137289|author1=Bird, D.M. |author2=Wright, J.O. |year=1977|title=Apparent distraction display by a Barred Owl|journal=Canadian Field-Naturalist |volume=91|issue=2 |pages= 176–177|doi=10.5962/p.345368 |doi-access=free}}</ref>

[[File:Barred-owl-chick-58.jpg|thumb|A chick nuzzles its mother after leaving its nest]]

The average hatchling weighs about {{convert|46|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Bent /><ref name= Elderkin /> Like most birds, the young are initially [[Altriciality|altricial]]. The white [[Down (feather)|down]] feathers that the barred owls are hatched with is replaced by white-tipped barred-buff second down at two to three weeks of age, correspondingly with growth of the wing primaries.<ref name= BNA /> The earlier chicks are bigger and stronger, while the latter ones may not survive if food is scarce.<ref name= Hume /> Size differences between barred owl nestlings may be less pronounced if the female does not begin incubation until the laying of the second egg.<ref name= BNA /> The larger young may fight with the younger siblings for food.<ref name= Hume /> The young barred owls first start moving about the nest at around this 3 week point and may start to perform threat displays if scared.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Varchmin>Varchmin, T. E. M. (1977). ''The Behavioral and Physical Development of Barred Owl (Strix Varia) Nestlings in Illinois'' (Doctoral dissertation, Western Illinois University).</ref> Adult-like feathers begin to appear at six weeks of age, starting at the scapulars, then radiating down across the abdomen and flanks up through the upper breast, with the last wisps of down remaining for up to 4 months.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Bent /><ref name= Varchmin /> The young often start exploring around the nest tree, often falling to ground, which makes them quite vulnerable to predation despite the parents' continued protection. However, the fledglings can usually clamber back up the tree using their feet and bills, constantly wing-flapping.<ref name= Elderkin />

Fledging occurs at about 36–39 days.<ref name= Voous /> After that, both parents continue to hunt for the young but prey deliveries taper off as the young begin to make their first flights and practice hunting.<ref name= Hume /><ref name= Dunstan /><ref name= Varchmin /><ref>[http://www.smm.org/warnernaturecenter/animals/owl/ ''Barred Owl Factsheet''] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150428114551/http://www.smm.org/warnernaturecenter/animals/owl/ |date=2015-04-28 }}, Lee and Rose Warner Nature Center, 2008</ref> Short flights are typical when the young barred owls are up to around 10 weeks old with longer flights commencing at about 14 weeks.<ref name= Varchmin /> By the time they are flying and hunting somewhat assuredly in early to mid-autumn, the young move away and gain independence.<ref name= Hume /><ref name= Johnsgard /> Dispersal of 5 young from Nova Scotia was {{convert|0.8|to|64|km|mi|abbr=on}} away from their nests of origin. Furthermore, a recovered 6 month old was {{convert|8|km|mi|abbr=on}} away from its nest of origin, while five encountered as adults were found at a distance of {{convert|16|and|43|km|mi|abbr=on}} from the nests where they hatched. An exceptional dispersal distance from Nova Scotia was {{convert|1600|km|mi|abbr=on}} away to [[Stoney Creek, Ontario]].<ref name= Elderkin /> The post-dispersal stage is one of great mortality among young owls.<ref name= Hume /> Usually, juvenile owls are considered "floaters", often hovering around the perimeter of fiercely defended territories for some time. In some areas, floaters may make use of nest boxes more readily than mature birds.<ref name= Nicholls /><ref name= Elderkin /> Yearlings have been known to breed only very rarely, otherwise barred owl usually first breed when they are around two (sometimes three) years old. Therefore, around two years old is considered the age of maturity.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Hybridization>{{cite journal|doi=10.2307/4088616|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v111n02/p0487-p0492.pdf|title=Hybridization Between Barred and Spotted Owls|journal=The Auk|date=April 1994 |volume=111|issue=2|pages=487–492|author1=Hamer, Thomas E. |author2=Forsman, Eric D.|jstor=4088616 }}</ref> The percentages of successful nests (i.e. at least 1 fledgling produced per attempt), per study were found to be: 86% of 22 clutches in Minnesota (with a mean of 2.42 fledglings per successful attempt),<ref name= Johnson2 /> 66% of 114 clutches in Michigan (with a mean of 1.97 fledglings per successful attempt),<ref name= Postupalsky /> 25% of 6 clutches in Maryland (with a mean of 1 fledgling per all attempts and 1.48 per successful attempts),<ref name= Devereux /> 69% of 48 clutches in Nova Scotia (with a mean of 0.25 fledgling per all attempts and 2 per successful attempts)<ref name= Elderkin /> and 50% of 12 nests in Washington state. Little is known about the specific factors that dictate breeding success, but are likely to include the quality of the nesting site, the food supply in the area, the levels of disturbance from outside actors (usually humans) and the maturity of the pair.

== Longevity and parasitism ==
The barred owl is a naturally long-living bird. Some record lifespans recorded per banding studies in the wild are 18 years 2 months (initial banding [[Kentucky]], died in [[Ohio]]) and 24 years and 1 month in Minnesota.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Klimkiewicz, M. K.|author2= Futcher, A. G. |year=1987|title=Longevity Records of North American Birds: Coerebinae through Estrildidae|jstor=4513247|journal=Journal of Field Ornithology|pages= 318–333|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jfo/v058n03/p0318-p0333.pdf|volume=58|issue=3}}</ref><ref name= Orfinger>{{cite journal|title=Longevity of the Barred Owl (''Strix varia'' Barton, 1799) from captivity|date=2018 |doi=10.1676/1559-4491.130.4.1009 |last1=Orfinger |first1=Alexander B. |last2=Helsel |first2=Debbie |last3=Breeding |first3=Shawnlei F. |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |volume=130 |issue=4 |page=1009 |s2cid=196672746 }}</ref> There are several records of breeding barred owls nesting successfully for a decade or more.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Johnsgard /> The record lifespan for a barred owl in captivity, where many animals can live longer without the stresses of surviving in wild conditions, was 34 years and 1 month, with six records of captive barred owls living over 30 years.<ref name= Orfinger /> Known causes of mortality are diverse, some due to predation (largely great horned owls, for nestlings to adults, and probably raccoons, for eggs, nestlings and fledglings).<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Johnsgard /> Some mortality is known to occur during hunting accidents.<ref name= BNA /> There are cases of barred owls being inadvertently killed in [[Trapping|furbearer traps]].<ref>{{cite journal|jstor=3782498|author1=Stocek, R. F.|author2=Cartwright, D. J. |year=1985|title=Birds as nontarget catches in the New Brunswick furbearer harvest|journal=Wildlife Society Bulletin|volume= 13|issue=3|pages= 314–317}}</ref> Sometimes deaths occurs due to the defenses of prey.<ref name= Voous /> One barred owl was killed by the bite of an unknown rodent that was likely defending itself.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Gibson, M. J.|author2=Gibson, D. C.|author3= Bardelmeier, D.
