== Status ==
[[File:Barred Owl Skeleton.jpg|thumb|Barred owl skeleton ([[Museum of Osteology]])]]

The barred owl is one of the most common owls in North America.<ref name= Hume /> [[Partners in Flight]] estimates that the barred owl may number up to 3 million individuals globally, making it, perhaps, the second most numerous North American owl behind the [[great horned owl]] and perhaps slightly ahead of other commoner species like [[American barn owl]]s and [[northern saw-whet owl]]s.<ref name= AllAbout>{{cite web |url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Barred_Owl/lifehistory |title=Barred Owl, Life History |author=The Cornell Lab of Ornithology |publisher=allaboutbirds}}</ref> It estimated that Canada, in total, holds about 10,000–50,000 pairs.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1016/S0006-3207(97)00051-7 |title=Population status and recent trends in Canadian raptors: A review |date=1998 |last1=Kirk |first1=David A. |last2=Hyslop |first2=Colleen |journal=Biological Conservation |volume=83 |issue=1 |pages=91–118 |bibcode=1998BCons..83...91K }}</ref> [[The Maritimes|Canadian Maritimes]] had highest densities known, with 3600 pairs, being found in 80% of {{convert|10|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} blocks totaling 377; this was a conspicuously higher density than that recorded in Ontario, where they were in 28% of 1824 study blocks.<ref name= Sauer>{{cite journal|doi=10.3996/nafa.79.0001 |title=The North American Breeding Bird Survey 1966–2011: Summary Analysis and Species Accounts |date=2013 |last1=Sauer |first1=John R. |last2=Link |first2=William A. |last3=Fallon |first3=Jane E. |last4=Pardieck |first4=Keith L. |last5=Ziolkowski |first5=David J. |journal=North American Fauna |volume=79 |pages=1–32 |url=https://digital.library.unt.edu/ark:/67531/metadc700932/ }}</ref> Iowa, Tennessee and Maryland had the highest densities of pairs apparently in the United States, being present from 39 to 47% of blocks.<ref name= Sauer /> Throughout the year, the mean number of sightings per route is 0.12 sightings while during [[Christmas Bird Count]]s (CBC), the mean in 0.25 per route.<ref name= Sauer /><ref>Sauer, J. R., Schwartz S., & Hoover, B. (1996). ''The Christmas Bird Count homepage. Version 95.1''. Patuxent Wildlife Research Center, Laurel, MD.</ref>

== Barred owl and northern spotted owl ==
[[File:Barred owl.jpg|thumb|left|[[Gatineau Park]], [[Gatineau]], [[Quebec]]]]

Barred owls are thought to be partly responsible for the recent decline of the [[northern spotted owl]], native to British Columbia, [[Washington (state)|Washington]], [[Oregon]], and [[California]].<ref>{{cite journal|author=Livezey, K.B.|year= 2005|title= Iverson (2004) on Spotted Owls and Barred Owls: comments on methods and conclusion|journal= Journal of Raptor Research |volume=39|pages=102–103|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/54163}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=Kelly, E.G. |author2=Forsman, E.D. |author3=Anthony, R.G. |title=Are Barred Owls displacing Spotted Owls?|journal=The Condor|volume=105|page=45|doi=10.1650/0010-5422(2003)105[45:ABODSO]2.0.CO;2|url=https://www.fws.gov/oregonfwo/species/data/northernspottedowl/BarredOwl/Documents/KellyEtAl2003.pdf|year=2003|s2cid=86547489 }}</ref> The 2011 Revised Recovery Plan for the Northern Spotted Owl states "Based on the best available scientific information, competition from the barred owl (''S. varia'') poses a significant and complex threat to the spotted owl."<ref>[https://www.fws.gov/sites/default/files/documents/NSO_RevisedRP_2011.pdf Revised Recovery Plan for the Northern Spotted Owl (''Strix occidentalis caurina'')]. U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, Portland, Oregon. (2011) p. vi</ref> Northern spotted owls were thought to be already declining considerably before barred owls moved into their range, mostly in sync with large-scale logging operations and land development carried out by humans, with their problems now further exacerbated by the barred species's presence.<ref name= Buchanan/> There are ecological discrepancies between the species in areas of sympatry. While both species prefer old-growth forest areas, the spotted owls tend to live in significantly higher elevation areas in Washington's Cascade mountains, with nest sites averaging {{convert|966.2|m|ft|abbr=on}} [[meters above sea level]] against a mean of {{convert|54.1|m|ft|abbr=on}} for barred owls, in areas with more steep slopes.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Pearson, R. R.|author2= Livezey, K. B. |year=2003|title=Distribution, numbers, and site characteristics of spotted owls and barred owls in the Cascade Mountains of Washington|url=https://biostor.org/reference/216275|journal= Journal of Raptor Research|volume= 37|issue=4|pages= 265–276}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|author1=Buchanan, J.|author2= Fleming, T.|author3= Irwin, L. |year=2004|title=A comparison of Barred and Spotted Owl nest-site characteristics in the Eastern Cascade Mountains, Washington|url=https://biostor.org/reference/216328|journal=  Journal of Raptor Research|volume= 38|pages= 231–237}}</ref> The barred owls of the area additionally prefer wetter, more variable forest areas, down to [[riparian zone]]s with limited forested land, while the spotted tended to cluster in more homogeneous upland and ancient [[Pinophyta|coniferous forests]].<ref name= Herter>{{cite journal|author1=Herter, D. R.|author2= Hicks, L. L. |year=2000|title=Barred owl and spotted owl populations and habitat in the central Cascade Range of Washington|url=https://biostor.org/reference/216080|journal= Journal of Raptor Research|volume= 34|issue=4|pages= 279–286}}</ref> However, little is known on the feasibility or benefits of habitat maintenance that favors spotted over barred owls. Habitat management measures are hypothesized to be insufficient to mitigate the negative effect the barred owls are having.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Livezey, K.B.|author2= Fleming, T.L. |year=2009|title=Effects of Barred Owls on Spotted Owls: The need for more than incidental detections and correlational analysis|journal=  Journal of Raptor Research|volume= 41|issue= 4 |pages= 319–325|doi= 10.3356/0892-1016(2007)41[319:EOBOOS]2.0.CO;2 |s2cid= 86757049 |doi-access= free}}</ref><ref>{{ Cite journal | issue = 6 | year = 2019 | volume = 83 | pages = 1281–1296 | last1 = Long | first1 = L. | last2 = Wolfe | first2 = J. |journal=Journal of Wildlife Management | url = https://www.fs.usda.gov/psw/publications/long/psw_2019_long001.pdf | s2cid = 201192768 | id = {{ S2CID | 221379957 }} | title = Review of the effects of barred owls on spotted owls | doi = 10.1002/jwmg.21715 | bibcode = 2019JWMan..83.1281L }}. This review cites Mangan, A. O. (2018) [https://ir.library.oregonstate.edu/concern/graduate_thesis_or_dissertations/4q77fw91b Effects of habitat characteristics, weather and presence of Barred Owls (''Strix varia'') on occupancy dynamics and breeding propensity of Northern Spotted Owls (''S. occidentalis caurina'') in Mount Rainier National Park]. Master's thesis. Oregon State University {{ S2CID | 199893769 }}</ref> In areas where barred owls moved in within {{convert|0.8|km|mi|abbr=on}} of a spotted owl nest area, 39% of spotted owls disappeared and were not seen again, while in areas still free of barred owls (so spared from this interspecific pressure), 11% of spotted owls disappeared and were not found again.<ref name= Kelly />

[[File:Barred Owl (Audubon).jpg|thumb|[[John James Audubon|Audubon]]'s illustration of a barred owl attacking a [[squirrel]]]]
Since the 1960s, barred owls have been expanding their range westward from the eastern US and Canada.<ref>Gremel, S. [https://web.archive.org/web/20110613203450/http://www.nature.nps.gov/YearInReview/yir2000/pages/02_nps_science/02_03_gremel.html ''Barred Owl Displaces Northern Spotted Owl at Olympic'']. Natural Resources Year in Review, U.S. National Park System.</ref> While some authors have described the expansion of barred owls into the west as "natural",<ref>Monahan, W. B., & Hijmans, R. J. (2007). ''Distributional dynamics of invasion and hybridization by Strix spp. in western North America''. Ornithological Monographs, 55–66.</ref> others state that this is a mischaracterization, as there is little to suggest that the barred owls could have reached the spotted's range without the inadvertent aid of humans — increasing temperatures, wildfire suppression, and tree planting likely facilitated the range increase.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1525/auk.2008.125.1.230 |title=Natural range expansion of Barred Owls? A critique of Monahan and Hijmans (2007) |date=2008 |last1=Livezey |first1=Kent B. |last2=Root |first2=Terry L. |last3=Gremel |first3=Scott A. |last4=Johnson |first4=Craig |journal=The Auk |volume=125 |pages=230–232 |s2cid=86273621 |doi-access=free }}</ref> When spotted owls and barred owls share the same area, the barred owls generally are more aggressive and out-compete the spotted owls, leading to decreased populations of the native owls.<ref>{{cite journal|url=http://www.specifysoftware.org/Informatics/bios/biostownpeterson/PR_CB_2003.pdf |title=Using Ecological-Niche Modeling to Predict Barred Owl Invasions with Implications for Spotted Owl Conservation |doi=10.1046/j.1523-1739.2003.02206.x |author=Peterson, A. Townsend |author2=Robins, C. Richard |name-list-style=amp|journal=Conservation Biology |pages=1161–1165 |volume=17 |issue=4 |year=2003 |bibcode=2003ConBi..17.1161P |s2cid=86528384 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080627040936/http://www.specifysoftware.org/Informatics/bios/biostownpeterson/PR_CB_2003.pdf |archive-date=2008-06-27 }}</ref> The more aggressive response of barred owls to interspecific stimuli has been verified with experiments incorporating vocalization and owl dummies.<ref>Van Lanen, N. J. (2010). ''Out with the old and in with the new?: investigating competition between Barred Owls (Strix varia) and Northern Spotted Owls (Strix occidentalis caurina) in northwestern California with a playback experiment''. Doctoral dissertation, Colorado State University. {{hdl|10217/38187}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1016/j.biocon.2011.05.011 |title=Who hits and hoots at whom? Potential for interference competition between barred and northern spotted owls |date=2011 |last1=Van Lanen |first1=Nicholas J. |last2=Franklin |first2=Alan B. |last3=Huyvaert |first3=Kathryn P. |last4=Reiser |first4=Raoul F. |last5=Carlson |first5=Peter C. |journal=Biological Conservation |volume=144 |issue=9 |pages=2194–2201 |bibcode=2011BCons.144.2194V |s2cid=53326444 |url=http://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=2330&context=icwdm_usdanwrc |url-access=subscription }}</ref> Additionally, the adult survival rates appear higher for barred owls (an estimated 91%) than for spotted owls (an estimated 82%). Therefore, the barred owl is considered "demographically superior".<ref name= Wiens /> In December 2023, the [[U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service]] issued a draft of a management plan indicating that hunters in the Pacific Northwest would be encouraged to shoot more than half a million barred owls. The rationale was to cull the numbers of this invasive species, to help protect native species, particularly the spotted owl which had been listed as threatened under the Endangered Species Act.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.newsweek.com/invasive-species-barred-owl-cull-pacific-northwest-1850348|title=US Government Wants Hunters to Shoot 500,000 Owls|date=December 7, 2023|work=Newsweek|access-date=December 10, 2023|quote=Barred owls are an invasive species in the Pacific Northwest, originating on the U.S. East Coast, and they pose a huge threat to native protected species, including northern spotted owls.}}</ref>

Another potential threat is that barred owls and spotted owls occasionally interbreed, creating hybrids ("sparred owl" or "botted owl").<ref name= Hybridization /><ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20021228024241/http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2002/12/1217_021226_tvinterbreeding.html ''Interbreeding Threatens Rare Species, Experts Claim''], Guynup, Sharon, National Geographic</ref> Only 47 hybrids with barred owls (all between female barred owls and male spotted owls) were found in an analysis of more than 9,000 banded spotted owls; consequently, hybridization between these two species is considered to be "an interesting biological phenomenon that is probably inconsequential compared with the real threat—direct competition between the two species for food and space".<ref name= Hybridization /><ref name="Kelly&Forsman2004">{{cite journal|vauthors=Kelly EG, Forsman ED|year= 2004|title= Recent records of hybridization between barred owls (''Strix varia'') and northern spotted owls (''S. occidentalis caurina'')|journal= Auk |doi=10.1642/0004-8038(2004)121[0806:RROHBB]2.0.CO;2|volume=121|issue= 3|pages=806–810|s2cid= 86769738}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/122898|author1=Seamans, M. E.|author2= Corcoran, J.|author3= Rex, A. |year=2004|title=Southernmost record of a spotted owl × barred owl hybrid in the Sierra Nevada|journal=Western Birds|volume= 35|pages= 173–174}}</ref> Most hybrids favor their barred owl heritage with similar and more pale overall coloring than spotted owls, though the back tends to be intermediate in color between the two and the beak coloring is variable. However, there has been much variation described.<ref name= BNA /><ref name= Hybridization /> Due to variances in appearance, previously hybrids between the species were overestimated from field identification, only being certainly identified by genetic [[Locus (genetics)|loci]].<ref>{{cite journal|title=Microsatellite loci for distinguishing spotted owls (''Strix occidentalis''), barred owls (''Strix varia''), and their hybrids|date=2007 |doi=10.1111/j.1471-8286.2006.01581.x |last1=Funk |first1=W. Chris |last2=Mullins |first2=Thomas D. |last3=Forsman |first3=Eric D. |last4=Haig |first4=Susan M. |journal=Molecular Ecology Notes |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=284–286 |url=https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/usgsstaffpub/687 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1111/j.1523-1739.2004.00206.x |title=Genetic Identification of Spotted Owls, Barred Owls, and Their Hybrids: Legal Implications of Hybrid Identity |date=2004 |last1=Haig |first1=Susan M. |last2=Mullins |first2=Thomas D. |last3=Forsman |first3=Eric D. |last4=Trail |first4=Pepper W. |last5=Wennerberg |first5=LIV |journal=Conservation Biology |volume=18 |issue=5 |pages=1347–1357 |bibcode=2004ConBi..18.1347H |s2cid=43989899 |url=https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1679&context=usgsstaffpub |url-access=subscription }}</ref> Moreover, no recent hybrids could be detected in a survey of the [[Cascade Mountains]].<ref name= Herter /><ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1534/g3.118.200754|title=Whole-Genome Analysis of Introgression Between the Spotted Owl and Barred Owl (''Strix occidentalis'' and ''Strix varia'', Respectively; Aves: Strigidae) in Western North America|date=2018 |last1=Hanna |first1=Zachary R. |last2=Dumbacher |first2=John P. |last3=Bowie |first3=Rauri C K. |last4=Henderson |first4=James B. |last5=Wall |first5=Jeffrey D. |journal=G3: Genes, Genomes, Genetics |volume=8 |issue=12 |pages=3945–3952 |pmid=30355766 |pmc=6288836 }}</ref><ref>Hanna, Z. R. (2017). [https://escholarship.org/uc/item/2nh1474g Genomics of the Spotted Owl (''Strix occidentalis'') and Barred Owl (''Strix varia'') in Western North America]. Doctoral dissertation, UC Berkeley.</ref>

== In art ==
[[John James Audubon]] illustrated the barred owl in ''[[Birds of America (book)|Birds of America]]'' (published in London, 1827–1838) as Plate 46, where it is shown threatening a [[eastern gray squirrel|grey squirrel]]. The image was engraved and colored by [[Robert Havell]]'s London workshops. The original [[aquatint]] by Audubon is owned by the Brooklyn Museum.<ref name="Brooklyn Museum">{{cite web|title=Brooklyn Museum|url=http://www.brooklynmuseum.org/opencollection/objects/84178/Barred_Owl|work=Collections: American Art: Barred Owl}}</ref>

橫斑林鴞（學名：Strix varia）是鴟鴞科林鴞屬的一種大型貓頭鷹。


特徵

橫斑林鴞長44-46厘米，翼展101-115厘米。它們的面部淡色，眼睛周圍有深色的環。喙呈黃色，眼睛褐色。它們是美國東部唯一擁有褐色的眼睛的貓頭鷹，其他的都是黃色眼睛。頭部很圓，沒有耳羽。上身呈灰褐色，下身為淺色且有斑紋。胸部有白色橫斑。腳上長有羽毛。


分佈及棲息地
橫斑林鴞分佈在加拿大、美國東部及墨西哥的密林中繁殖，近年擴展至美國西部，具备领地意识。研究顯示郊區很適合它們棲息，它們在郊區的數量比在古老原始林的上升更快。它們在郊區的威脅只有車輛。


與北方斑點鴞的關係

橫斑林鴞對於在美國華盛頓州、加利福尼亞州及俄勒岡州的北方斑點鴞的衰落須負上部份責任。自1960年代，橫斑林鴞就向西擴展。當北方斑點鴞與橫斑林鴞在同一環境下，後者一般會具有攻擊性，令前者的數量減少。這兩種貓頭鷹也會混種。


行為及生態


繁殖
橫斑林鴞一般會在樹穴築巢，也會佔用烏鴉及松鼠的巢。它們是留鳥，但在繁殖季節後也會離開。當位點適合築巢，它們往往會重用此地方。在美國佛羅里達州南部的會在1月初生蛋，而在緬因州北部的則會在4月中生蛋，每次生2-4隻蛋。雌鳥會負責孵蛋，孵化約需4星期。雛鳥要4-5個星期大才會換羽。


食性
橫斑林鴞主要吃小鼠屬的多個物種，也會吃細小的哺乳動物及鳥類（如松雞科、鳩鴿科及鴨）。它們有時會涉水捕魚或龜鱉類。
橫斑林鴞會在夜間獵食，停留在高處等候獵物，或是在林間飛行搜尋獵物。它們是北美洲中在日間最活躍的貓頭鷹，尤其是在哺育雛鳥。
